turns.content: Why are African national borders so geometric while European ones follow rivers and mountains?

A big part of it is timing and process. Many European borders evolved gradually over centuries through wars, dynastic marriages, treaties, and shifting spheres of influence. That long, iterative history often “settled” along natural features that were easy to recognize and defend—ridges, rivers, marshes, and passes. In much of Africa, by contrast, a large share of modern international borders were formalized relatively quickly during late-19th- and early-20th-century colonial partition and administration. Colonial powers frequently drew lines on maps to define spheres of influence, concessions, or administrative districts before they had detailed surveys of the interior. Straight lines—especially meridians and parallels—were convenient because they were unambiguous on a map and could be described cleanly in treaties (“the 14th meridian east,” “the 22nd parallel north,” etc.). Later, when independence came, the Organization of African Unity (now the African Union) largely favored maintaining inherited colonial borders (the principle of uti possidetis) to reduce the risk of endless border wars. It’s not that Europe lacks straight borders or Africa lacks natural boundaries; it’s more about the overall proportion and the historical mechanisms that produced them.
Can you break down which colonial conferences and treaties mattered most for that “drawn on a map” effect?

The most cited milestone is the Berlin Conference of 1884–1885, but it’s important to be precise about what it did and didn’t do. 1) Berlin Conference (1884–1885) - It did not literally draw all of Africa’s borders in a single meeting. - It set rules and principles for colonial claims, especially “effective occupation” (a power had to show some administrative presence to legitimize claims) and free navigation on major rivers like the Congo and Niger. - It helped accelerate the “Scramble for Africa” by formalizing a framework that made territorial claims more systematic and competitive. 2) Bilateral treaties among European powers (1880s–1910s) Many actual boundary lines came from bilateral agreements: - Anglo-French agreements (e.g., 1898–1899 arrangements after the Fashoda incident; 1904 Entente Cordiale clarifying spheres) - Anglo-German agreements (before WWI, defining boundaries of German colonies like Togoland and Kamerun) - Franco-German agreements (e.g., in Central Africa) - Anglo-Portuguese treaties affecting borders around Angola, Mozambique, and adjacent territories These treaties often used geometric definitions (parallels/meridians) for expedience, especially where the interior was poorly mapped. Where major rivers were known, treaties sometimes used thalwegs (the deepest channel line) or river courses as boundaries. 3) Colonial boundary commissions and demarcation (early 1900s onward) After “paper borders” were agreed, commissions attempted to demarcate them on the ground. Where the treaty said “the 10th parallel,” surveyors would place boundary markers along an abstract line—hence long straight segments. In other places, they would interpret vague language (“the watershed”) into a line that could be more irregular. 4) Post-independence legal consolidation (1960s onward) The OAU’s 1964 Cairo Resolution emphasized respect for inherited borders. This didn’t create the straightness, but it kept those lines from being renegotiated on a large scale. So: Berlin set the competitive rules; bilateral treaties often supplied the straight-line text; demarcation commissions converted that text into concrete boundaries; and post-independence norms stabilized them.
People often say Europeans used rivers and mountains in Europe because they’re “natural,” but in Africa they ignored them. Is that actually true, or just a simplified story?

It’s simplified. Europe: - Many European borders do follow rivers (Rhine segments, Danube segments, Oder-Neisse) or mountain ridges (Pyrenees, Alps in parts). - But Europe also has plenty of “non-natural” borders: straight-ish segments from treaties, enclaves/exclaves, and lines reflecting historical feudal holdings. The Belgium–Netherlands border is famously intricate; parts of Germany’s borders reflect political compromises rather than pure physical geography; and the internal borders that later became international (e.g., within the Holy Roman Empire’s legacy) often had little to do with topography. Africa: - Many African borders do use rivers and lakes: the Congo River in parts, the Niger in segments, the Senegal River, the Zambezi in places, Lake Tanganyika, Lake Malawi/Nyasa, Lake Victoria shorelines, etc. - Major deserts and ecological zones also influenced boundaries indirectly (e.g., the Sahara as a barrier to control, which shaped colonial reach and spheres). - The “ignored natural features” narrative is partly about mismatches: a geometric line can cut across watersheds, grazing routes, linguistic regions, and precolonial political networks. But in many regions there weren’t single dominant mountain barriers comparable to the Pyrenees or Alps that would have offered obvious boundary lines. In parts of Africa the interior is plateau or savanna with gradual transitions rather than sharp ridges. Why the difference in impression: - Map aesthetics: Straight lines stand out visually. - Scale: Africa is large; long straight segments can run hundreds of kilometers and become iconic. - Survey knowledge: In Europe, rivers and mountain chains were well mapped early; in Africa, many interior areas were mapped later, and colonial treaties were sometimes concluded before detailed ground knowledge. So it’s not that Africa is uniquely “unnatural” and Europe uniquely “natural.” It’s more that the mechanisms and timing of boundary-making differed, and the resulting cartographic style differs.
Could you explain more about the technical side of straight-line borders—like parallels/meridians—and why they were attractive legally?

Parallels and meridians are attractive because they’re definable in a coordinate system that can be referenced in text. 1) Precision in treaty language A treaty can say: “The boundary shall follow the 11th parallel north from point A to point B.” That’s concise and, in principle, unambiguous. 2) Avoiding disputed geographic features If you define a border as “the river,” you must specify which branch, what happens if the river shifts, where the mouth is, whether islands belong to one side, and whether the boundary follows the thalweg or the midline. Rivers meander and can change course. Mountains can have multiple ridges and ambiguous watersheds. A geometric line seems simpler. 3) Mapping and administration Colonial administrations wanted manageable districts. A straight line can be easy to draw on a map for tax, concession, or military zones, even if it’s not easy to patrol on the ground. The goal was often administrative clarity for the colonizer rather than local coherence. 4) Surveying and geodesy (with caveats) In the late 19th century, geodetic surveying existed but was uneven. Determining an exact meridian on the ground required astronomical observations and later triangulation networks; it wasn’t trivial. Still, it was conceptually straightforward compared with negotiating every bend in a river or every ridge line. 5) Legal stability over time (again, in theory) A coordinate-based line is stable even if the landscape changes. Rivers move; coastlines erode; lakes shrink. A meridian doesn’t move. In practice, geometric borders still generated disputes because: - Early maps had errors. - Different datums and reference meridians could be used. - Demarcation on the ground could drift due to measurement uncertainty. - Local geography forced practical deviations (e.g., to avoid bisecting a village). But as a legal drafting device, parallels and meridians were appealing.
You mentioned rivers shifting. In Europe, how do borders handle that? And did Africa’s river borders cause similar issues?

River-border law has a fairly developed set of principles in international practice, though each treaty can specify exceptions. Key concepts: 1) Thalweg principle - Often used on navigable rivers: the boundary follows the thalweg (deepest channel / main navigable channel). - This can matter where a river has multiple channels or islands. 2) Median line principle - Sometimes used on non-navigable rivers: boundary follows the midline between banks. 3) Accretion vs avulsion - Accretion: gradual, natural change in a river’s course. Many legal traditions treat the boundary as moving with the river under accretion. - Avulsion: sudden change (e.g., flood cutting a new channel). Often the boundary stays in the old channel line to avoid abrupt territorial shifts. Europe examples: - The Rhine has long-standing boundary segments with detailed treaties and commissions (Central Commission for Navigation on the Rhine is a related institution, though not solely about borders). - The Danube has multiple international segments with treaty regimes. - The Oder-Neisse line after WWII is a political boundary using rivers, with specific arrangements. Africa: - Similar issues arise. If a border is “the river,” then islands, channels, and shifting courses matter. - The Senegal River has had cooperative management mechanisms. - The Congo River’s islands and channels have been relevant in DRC–Republic of the Congo relations. - The Kasikili/Sedudu Island dispute (Botswana–Namibia) hinged on interpreting the “main channel” of the Chobe River; the ICJ decided in 1999 based on which channel was the main one. So river borders can be stable if well managed, but they require continuous cooperation and clear rules. Straight-line borders avoid some hydrological complexity but introduce other problems (e.g., cutting across communities and land-use patterns).
What about the famous straight borders specifically—can you name a few and how they were defined?

Several well-known examples illustrate different drafting logics: 1) Egypt–Sudan (partly) - The political boundary along the 22nd parallel north is a classic geometric line. - Complicated by the Hala’ib Triangle and Bir Tawil anomalies, which stem from different administrative vs political boundary interpretations in the early 1900s. 2) Algeria–Mali–Niger–Libya (segments) - Large Saharan stretches include long straight segments, reflecting colonial-era definitions in sparsely populated desert where control was projected more on maps than on the ground. 3) Chad–Libya (Aouzou Strip context) - The Chad–Libya dispute involved colonial-era treaties and ambiguous delimitations in the Sahara; the ICJ ruled in 1994 in favor of Chad based on treaty interpretation. 4) Namibia–Botswana–Zambia–Zimbabwe area (the “Caprivi Strip” region) - While not purely straight-line in all parts, the Caprivi Strip itself is a corridor-like shape tied to German colonial aims to reach the Zambezi, showing how strategic considerations shaped borders. 5) Kenya–Uganda and Tanzania–Kenya (some segments) - Not as iconic as Sahara lines, but there are straight-ish segments and lake boundaries (Lake Victoria) that required delimitation. 6) Mauritania–Mali and Mali–Niger (segments) - Straight lines in the Sahel/Sahara reflect French colonial administrative limits. These lines often were defined by: - Latitude/longitude lines; - Lines between “points” (e.g., from one well or hill to another) even if those points were poorly fixed; - Watershed lines (more irregular, but sometimes treated abstractly).
The Egypt–Sudan situation is confusing. Can you explain how a straight parallel created those odd territories?

The core is that two different boundary concepts were applied at different times: 1) 1899 “Political” boundary - Britain and Egypt (under British influence) set the boundary at the 22nd parallel north: everything south was Sudan; north was Egypt. 2) 1902 “Administrative” boundary adjustments - For practical administration, Britain adjusted which authority administered certain areas based on local ties and access. - The Hala’ib Triangle (north of the 22nd parallel) was placed under Sudanese administration because it was easier to administer from Sudan. - Bir Tawil (south of the 22nd parallel) was placed under Egyptian administration for similar reasons. After Sudan’s independence, each state emphasized the boundary version that favored its claim: - Egypt emphasizes the 1899 political boundary (22nd parallel), claiming Hala’ib. - Sudan emphasizes the 1902 administrative boundary, also claiming Hala’ib. Because both claim Hala’ib, neither claims Bir Tawil (doing so would undermine their preferred argument). Hence Bir Tawil is an unclaimed territory in practice, while Hala’ib is disputed and effectively controlled by Egypt today. This shows how even a simple geometric line can become complicated when administrative practice diverges from treaty text.
That makes sense. Switching to Europe: are there cases where Europe used geometric borders too, especially in colonies or even within Europe?

Yes, both. Within Europe: - The border between Norway and Sweden has segments influenced by watershed and negotiated lines, but also some straighter stretches. - The Spain–Portugal border (the “Raya”) is historically old and often follows rivers, but also includes straight segments and treaty-defined lines. - The modern borders in parts of Central/Eastern Europe were redrawn in the 20th century with political criteria; some segments are relatively straight, though often less dramatically so than Saharan lines. In colonial contexts (outside Europe but decided by European powers): - North America: the U.S.–Canada border includes the 49th parallel for long stretches, a classic geometric border. - The Middle East: some borders (e.g., Iraq–Jordan–Saudi Arabia historically) had straight segments from British/French agreements, though later adjustments occurred. - Australia’s internal state borders often follow meridians/parallels, reflecting colonial administrative convenience. So geometric borders aren’t uniquely African; they’re a feature of statecraft when drawing boundaries across large, sparsely mapped or sparsely settled areas, especially in colonial settings.
So why do people fixate on Africa as the example, not North America or Australia?

A few reasons: 1) Density of straight segments relative to the continent’s political map Africa has many states and many borders; several prominent ones are long and straight, especially across desert and savanna. 2) Historical narrative salience The “Scramble for Africa” is a well-known emblem of colonial partition. Straight lines become a visual shorthand for external imposition. 3) Contrast with precolonial political geography Many African societies had political systems where authority gradients were more common than hard lines, and borders could be zones rather than precise lines. When modern cartographic borders were fixed, the contrast seemed stark. 4) Postcolonial consequences Some African borders cut across linguistic groups, pastoral routes, and ecological zones, contributing to disputes or internal tensions (though it’s never the only factor). That gives the border story ongoing relevance. 5) Map projection and education World maps used in classrooms often show Africa with bold straight borders. North American straight borders are also known (49th parallel), but the narrative focus tends to be on U.S.–Canada as a stable case rather than a cautionary tale.
You mentioned borders being “zones” historically. Can you explain that idea without getting too abstract?

Think of a border as either: - a line: a precise, surveyed demarcation where sovereignty changes at a specific point; or - a frontier zone: a region where influence fades gradually, and control is negotiated, contested, or shared. In many premodern settings worldwide (not just Africa), states didn’t always have the administrative capacity to enforce a crisp line across every kilometer. Instead, there might be: - buffer areas between kingdoms; - tribute relationships that varied by season or political circumstance; - overlapping claims (e.g., a community pays tribute to one polity but trades with another); - pastoral corridors used by multiple groups. Colonial administrations often sought line boundaries because they were needed for: - taxation; - concession grants (mining, plantations); - military jurisdiction; - diplomatic clarity between rival colonial powers. So when a frontier zone is converted into a line, it can cut through complex patterns that had been managed more flexibly.
How did actual surveying work in places with a “straight line” treaty? Did they literally march along a latitude with instruments?

In principle, yes: to demarcate a parallel or meridian, surveyors needed to determine geographic coordinates repeatedly along the intended line and place markers. Typical methods (late 19th to early 20th century): 1) Astronomical observations - Latitude could be determined by observing the altitude of known stars or the sun at local noon. - Longitude was harder historically; it required accurate timekeeping (chronometers) and astronomical observations, or later radio time signals. 2) Triangulation networks - Surveyors would establish a baseline and then create a chain of triangles between visible points (hilltops, constructed beacons). - This could provide a framework to position points with increasing accuracy. 3) Traverse surveys - In flatter terrain, surveyors might do a traverse with compass bearings and measured distances, though this accumulates error. 4) Boundary pillars - Once the line was agreed, physical markers (cairns, pillars) were placed at intervals and at turning points. - In deserts, pillars could be destroyed or buried; maintenance was an issue. Practical complications: - Terrain and logistics: water supply, disease, and security affected where survey teams could go. - Visibility: triangulation needs line-of-sight; dense vegetation complicates it. - Datum differences: the “latitude” depends on the reference ellipsoid and datum; older surveys used different assumptions about Earth’s shape. So even a “simple” straight line could take years to demarcate and could still be contested later if the original coordinate framework was uncertain.
Are there examples where a straight border is actually not that straight on the ground because of these surveying errors?

Yes. On small-scale maps, a border may appear perfectly straight, but on the ground it can have subtle kinks and offsets due to: - errors in early surveying; - later re-demarcations using different equipment; - practical deviations to accommodate settlements or terrain. Examples (general patterns rather than one single famous case): - Some Sahara/Sahel boundaries that are nominally along a parallel have minor deviations where boundary pillars were placed based on best-available measurements at the time. - In southern Africa, segments intended as straight can show small zigzags when mapped at high resolution because demarcation followed a sequence of pillars rather than a continuously computed geodesic. - Even the U.S.–Canada 49th parallel has deviations in places due to early survey limitations. Also, there’s a geometric nuance: on a sphere/ellipsoid, the shortest path between two points (a geodesic) isn’t the same as a line of constant bearing (loxodrome) or a line of constant latitude. When treaties say “straight line” between points, how that’s interpreted can vary: is it a geodesic, a rhumb line, or a line drawn on a particular map projection? Modern boundary law often clarifies this, but older treaties sometimes didn’t.
That’s interesting—so “straight” depends on projection. Can you explain that with a concrete example?

Sure. Imagine two boundary points, A and B, far apart. - On a Mercator projection map, a straight line drawn with a ruler represents a rhumb line: a path of constant compass bearing. That’s useful for navigation but not the shortest path. - On a globe, the shortest path is a great-circle route (or geodesic on an ellipsoid). On Mercator, that great-circle route usually appears curved (except along the equator or meridians). - A line of constant latitude (a parallel) appears straight on many cylindrical projections, but on the globe it’s a small circle (except the equator). So if a treaty says “a straight line from point A to point B,” and negotiators literally drew it on a Mercator map, the intended boundary might be a rhumb line. But if later surveyors interpret it as a geodesic, the line on the ground will differ slightly. In practice, many colonial treaties avoided this ambiguity by specifying: - “the x-th parallel” (constant latitude), - “the y-th meridian” (constant longitude), - or “a line joining point A (lat/long) to point B (lat/long).” But if they didn’t specify the geodetic method, later commissions had to interpret intent.
How common are borders based on watersheds? Those seem “natural” but also complicated.

Watershed (drainage divide) boundaries are common in mountainous or hilly regions because they can be defensible and conceptually clear: water flows one way on one side, another way on the other. Why they’re attractive: - In rugged terrain, divides are prominent and often coincide with ridgelines. - They can be stable over time (though erosion and landslides can alter micro-topography). - They avoid river-shift problems because the divide is not a moving channel. Why they’re complicated: - In plateau or gently rolling terrain, the divide can be subtle and hard to locate precisely. - Some basins are endorheic (internal drainage), complicating “which watershed” logic. - Human modifications (canals, diversions) can blur hydrological realities, though legal boundaries usually follow the natural divide as originally understood. In Africa, there are watershed-based borders in places with clearer highlands (e.g., parts of East Africa, Cameroon highlands, Ethiopian highlands regions affecting internal boundaries more than international). But in large low-relief areas, watersheds may not be a convenient line.
If Africa’s borders were stabilized at independence, were there any major attempts to redraw them to be more “natural” or ethnic-linguistic?

There were proposals and ideologies (like pan-Africanism, federations, or regional unions), but large-scale redrawing of borders was generally avoided because of the fear of cascading disputes. Reasons: - Every border change creates winners and losers; even if one change seems “rational,” it can trigger competing claims elsewhere. - Many populations are intermingled; drawing “ethnic borders” can be impossible without mass displacement. - Infrastructure, administrative centers, and economic networks had already been built around colonial boundaries. - Uti possidetis (keep inherited administrative boundaries as international borders) was seen as a pragmatic rule to prevent constant wars. That said, there have been significant changes: - Eritrea’s independence from Ethiopia (1993) created a new international border (and later a war over its demarcation). - South Sudan’s independence (2011) created a new state and border, with disputes (Abyei, etc.). - Western Sahara remains disputed. - Somaliland is de facto separate but not widely recognized. These are exceptions rather than a continent-wide border redesign project.
You keep saying “uti possidetis.” Can you define it clearly and how it applied in Africa?

Uti possidetis (more fully, uti possidetis juris) is a principle in international law meaning roughly: “as you possess under law, so you shall possess.” In decolonization contexts, it’s used to say that newly independent states should inherit the pre-independence administrative boundaries as their international borders. In Africa: - The OAU’s 1964 resolution endorsed respect for existing borders at independence. - The goal was stability: even if the borders were imperfect, changing them could produce widespread conflict. - Courts and arbitration have often relied on colonial-era maps, treaties, and administrative records when resolving African boundary disputes. It doesn’t mean borders can never change, but it sets a default presumption: keep them unless there’s mutual agreement or a legal ruling.
Does that mean colonial maps became legal evidence? How do you even use old maps in modern disputes?

Yes, old maps can become evidence, but courts treat them carefully. In boundary disputes, evidence can include: 1) Treaties and diplomatic correspondence - The primary sources: the text that created the boundary. 2) Administrative acts (“effectivités”) - Evidence of actual administration: tax collection, policing, local governance, issuance of permits, etc. This can matter if treaty text is vague. 3) Maps - Maps can illustrate intent, but their legal weight depends on context. - If a map is explicitly annexed to a treaty and agreed by both parties, it carries more weight. - If a map is unilateral, produced by one party, it’s weaker. 4) Demarcation records - Reports of boundary commissions, coordinates of pillars, survey notebooks. 5) Local usage - Where communities understood the border to be, though this is often secondary to treaty law. Courts often look for consistency: do multiple independent maps and administrative records align with a particular interpretation? They also consider the technical quality of the map: scale, date, projection, and known errors.
How did European borders become “naturalized” over time? Like, even if they weren’t originally natural, people now perceive them as such.

A few mechanisms make borders feel “natural” even if they started as political compromises: 1) Long-term stability If a border persists for centuries, infrastructure and institutions develop around it—roads, fortifications, customs posts historically, and administrative divisions. Over time it becomes part of the mental geography. 2) Cultural and linguistic consolidation States often promote a national language and identity. Over centuries, language boundaries can become sharper, making the political border seem to match a cultural boundary (even if it didn’t originally). 3) Selective memory and cartographic reinforcement School maps, atlases, and public narratives emphasize certain “natural” features as destiny (mountains as “frontiers”), even when the real history involved negotiation and war. 4) Environmental determinism narratives It’s tempting to say “the Pyrenees separate Spain and France,” which is partly true, but the exact line and its meaning are political. 5) Border management regimes In Europe, especially post-1945 and within the EU/Schengen, borders have changed functionally (less about defense, more about administration). This can paradoxically make the line feel like a benign, “natural” division rather than a contested frontier. So perception is shaped by time, institutions, and narratives, not just topography.
Are there African borders that *do* follow mountains in a way similar to the Pyrenees?

Yes, though they may be less globally famous. Examples of mountainous or highland-influenced boundaries: - Parts of the Algeria–Morocco boundary relate to the Atlas region (though the political dispute is more about colonial-era definitions and Western Sahara context). - The border between Uganda and the DRC runs through the Rwenzori Mountains region in places. - Rwanda–DRC and Uganda–DRC boundaries touch highland and volcanic regions around the Virunga Mountains, though lakes and colonial demarcations also matter. - Ethiopia’s borders with Eritrea and Djibouti involve escarpments and highland-to-lowland transitions, though the exact lines are political. - Lesotho is a clear case where the state’s territory is strongly tied to the Drakensberg/Maloti highlands; its boundary with South Africa roughly encircles a mountainous enclave, though it’s still a product of 19th-century political arrangements. So Africa has “natural feature” borders too; they’re just interspersed with many geometric segments elsewhere.
What about coastlines? Are maritime boundaries also “straight” and are they similar issues?

Maritime boundaries introduce a different geometry, often explicitly mathematical. Key points: 1) Territorial sea and EEZ concepts Under UNCLOS, states have entitlements like: - Territorial sea up to 12 nautical miles, - Contiguous zone, - Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) up to 200 nautical miles, - Continental shelf rights (sometimes beyond 200 nm). 2) Equidistance/median lines When coasts are adjacent or opposite and entitlements overlap, boundaries are often negotiated using equidistance lines (points equidistant from each coast), then adjusted for “relevant circumstances.” 3) Straight baselines (different meaning) States can use “straight baselines” along deeply indented coasts or fringing islands to measure maritime zones. This is not a border per se but a method for defining starting points. 4) Technical disputes Maritime delimitation can be highly technical: choice of basepoints, treatment of small islands, effect of coastal concavity, proportionality tests. In Africa, maritime boundaries have become increasingly important due to offshore oil/gas and fisheries (e.g., Gulf of Guinea). Some cases went to the ICJ or ITLOS, like Ghana–Côte d’Ivoire (ITLOS special chamber, 2017), which used an equidistance-based approach. So yes, maritime lines can look “straight” on maps because they’re constructed from geometric principles, but they’re different from colonial-era straight land borders.
Going back to land borders, can you explain the difference between delimitation and demarcation? People mix them up.

Delimitation and demarcation are related but distinct steps: - Delimitation: The legal definition of the boundary, usually in a treaty or legal decision. It’s the “paper boundary” described by text, coordinates, and sometimes maps. - Demarcation: The physical marking of that boundary on the ground—placing pillars, beacons, or other markers, and producing detailed demarcation maps and coordinate lists. A border can be delimited but not fully demarcated (common in remote areas). Lack of demarcation can increase local disputes (e.g., over grazing, policing) even if the legal line is agreed.
In Africa, were some borders left undemarcated at independence, and does that still matter today?

Yes. Many borders were only partially demarcated during colonial times, especially in remote deserts, dense forests, or sparsely populated areas. Why it still matters: - Resource discovery (oil, minerals, timber) can raise stakes in previously neglected regions. - Population growth can push settlement into frontier zones. - Security issues can make precise jurisdiction important. - Local communities may have customary land use that doesn’t match the assumed line. Many African states have worked—sometimes with AU support or bilateral commissions—to demarcate and manage borders more clearly. The AU Border Programme has encouraged delimitation/demarcation and cross-border cooperation, aiming to reduce conflict.
Can you discuss a concrete dispute where the straightness or vagueness of a colonial border caused problems later?

The Aouzou Strip dispute (Chad–Libya) is a good example of colonial-era ambiguity in a largely desert region. - The dispute centered on a strip in northern Chad. - Competing claims referenced different colonial-era agreements, including a 1955 France–Libya treaty (and earlier arrangements). - Libya occupied the area for periods, and conflict escalated in the late 20th century. - The ICJ in 1994 ruled largely in favor of Chad, emphasizing treaty interpretation and the validity of certain colonial-era instruments. While not only about “straightness,” it shows how lines negotiated by colonial powers—sometimes with incomplete geographic clarity—could become focal points for later interstate conflict. Another example emphasizing river interpretation rather than straightness is Kasikili/Sedudu Island (Botswana–Namibia), where colonial wording about the “main channel” mattered.
I’ve seen maps where a border is a straight line across the Sahara and people say “they just used a ruler.” Is that literally true?

Sometimes it’s close to literal in the sense that negotiators drew straight segments on small-scale maps to represent an intended boundary, especially when: - the area was poorly surveyed, - there were few settlements recognized by the colonial powers, - and the main goal was to separate spheres of influence. But even then, it wasn’t always casual. There could be: - diplomatic bargaining over strategic oases or caravan routes, - references to known points (wells, forts), - later survey efforts to translate the map line into coordinates and pillars. The “ruler” phrase is a simplification, but it captures a real phenomenon: straight-line boundaries were a practical cartographic and legal tool for dividing large areas quickly.
How does topography differ between Africa and Europe in a way that might also affect border shapes?

Topography contributes to the pattern, though it doesn’t determine it. Europe: - Has several prominent, continuous mountain systems (Pyrenees, Alps, Carpathians, Scandinavian Mountains) and many navigable rivers in relatively compact spaces. - High population density and intensive agriculture historically meant borders were negotiated in detail around valuable land and strategic passes. Africa: - Has major highlands and rift systems (Ethiopian Highlands, East African Rift, Atlas, Drakensberg), but also vast plateaus, savannas, and deserts where dramatic continuous barriers are less common or less relevant for state boundaries. - In the Sahara, for example, there are mountain massifs and oases, but huge expanses are relatively uniform in terms of obstacles—making geometric delimitation more tempting to distant negotiators. So the environment can make natural-feature boundaries more or less convenient. But the decisive factor remains political history and the colonial partition process.
Can you talk about the Sahel specifically? Many borders there look straight. Was that mostly French colonial administration?

A large portion of Sahelian borders were shaped by French colonial administration (French West Africa and French Equatorial Africa), but not exclusively—British and other colonial territories also intersect there. In the French case: - France organized vast territories into administrative units that changed over time. Internal administrative boundaries later became international borders at independence. - In sparsely populated areas, administrative convenience often meant using geometric lines or broad zones. - The Sahel’s ecology supports pastoralism and seasonal movement; colonial boundaries often didn’t align with those patterns, but the colonial state’s priority was administrative order, not matching mobility networks. Also, the Sahel was a zone where multiple colonial spheres met (French, British, German pre-WWI, and to some extent Italian in Libya), so straight lines sometimes represent negotiated compromises between empires.
If colonial borders were so arbitrary, why haven’t more of them been changed peacefully by agreement?

Some have been adjusted peacefully, but large-scale changes are rare because: 1) Risk of domino effects If one border is reopened, others may be contested, potentially destabilizing regions. 2) National identity and sovereignty Borders become tied to national narratives. Even if a border is historically arbitrary, changing it can be politically toxic. 3) Mixed populations Any “rational” redrawing can still leave minorities on the “wrong” side or create new ones. 4) Resource and infrastructure entanglement Oil fields, water access, roads, and administrative centers complicate any redraw. 5) International law defaults Uti possidetis creates a strong presumption of continuity. Peaceful changes require mutual consent, which is hard when stakes are high. 6) Internal politics Leaders may fear that conceding territory sets a precedent or is seen as weakness. So the stability norm, while preserving imperfect borders, has often been seen as the lesser evil.
Are there cases in Africa where borders were deliberately drawn to split groups, or was it more a byproduct?

It was more often a byproduct of imperial negotiation and administrative convenience than a consistent deliberate strategy to split specific groups everywhere. That said, colonial powers sometimes did apply “divide and rule” tactics within colonies—managing groups differently, favoring some intermediaries, etc.—but that’s not the same as systematically designing international borders to split ethnic groups. Border outcomes reflected: - rivalry between empires, - access to coasts and rivers, - control of trade routes, - claims based on exploration and treaties with local rulers (often contested), - administrative pragmatism. Splitting groups happened frequently because ethnolinguistic distributions rarely align with clean lines, and because colonial negotiators often prioritized inter-imperial clarity over local cohesion.
Can you compare Africa’s border-making to Latin America’s? They also had colonial boundaries that became national borders, right?

Yes, and Latin America is actually a useful comparison. Similarities: - Colonial administrative units became the basis for post-independence borders (also a form of uti possidetis). - Many disputes relied on interpreting old colonial documents, maps, and administrative acts. Differences: - Latin American independence occurred earlier (early 19th century), and many borders were contested and fought over during the 19th and early 20th centuries, leading to a long period of adjustment and settlement. - Some borders in Latin America follow natural features (Andes, major rivers) but others are geometric or based on colonial jurisdiction lines. - The scale and mapping knowledge varied; in Amazonia, borders were often vague for a long time, similar to parts of Central Africa. So the “colonial admin boundaries become international borders” pattern is global, not unique to Africa. The difference is that Africa’s decolonization wave was later and more compressed in time, with stronger continental norms favoring keeping borders fixed.
Good point. Back to the “straightness”: is there a way to quantify how straight a continent’s borders are?

Researchers have tried to quantify “border straightness” using metrics like: - Fraction of border length that follows lines of latitude/longitude or straight segments. - Sinuosity indices: ratio of actual border length to the length of a simplified line between endpoints (more sinuous = more “wiggly”). - Fractal dimension measures: how border complexity changes with measurement scale. - Proportion of borders following natural features (rivers, ridges) vs geometric lines. These measures depend on data resolution and definitions. A border that looks straight at 1:10 million scale may be quite jagged at 1:50,000 due to detailed demarcation around settlements or terrain. In general, Africa tends to score higher on geometric-segment proportion than Europe, but any comparison should control for: - map scale, - coastline complexity (which is not a border but affects perception), - number of states and border lengths.
If you had to choose, what’s the single biggest reason for the difference in perception between Africa and Europe?

The biggest reason is the compressed, externally negotiated colonial partition of much of Africa using cartographic and legal conveniences (parallels, meridians, straight segments) combined with post-independence preservation of those borders—versus Europe’s long, iterative boundary evolution with many opportunities for borders to be adjusted to terrain, settlements, and political realities.
Can we go region by region in Africa and talk about typical border patterns and why they look the way they do?

Yes. A regional sketch (with the caveat that every border has its own history): 1) North Africa (Mediterranean + Sahara) - Coastal areas: historical polities and colonial possessions created borders that can be irregular near populated zones. - Sahara interior: long straight segments are common due to sparse settlement, colonial spheres, and later treaty demarcations. Disputes like Western Sahara also shape the map. 2) West Africa - Many borders reflect French and British colonial divisions. - Rivers are used in places (Senegal River, Niger segments), but there are also straight lines, especially in Sahelian interiors. - Coastal borders can be more complex due to dense populations and colonial trading posts. 3) Central Africa - Dense forests and river basins: rivers matter (Congo system), but colonial concessions and administrative boundaries also produced straight segments. - The Congo Free State/Belgian Congo history is distinctive; boundaries were influenced by international recognition and river access. 4) East Africa / Great Lakes - More reliance on lakes and some highland features; Lake Victoria, Tanganyika, and others play roles. - The legacy of German East Africa vs British and Belgian territories shaped boundaries. - High population densities around the lakes make border management sensitive. 5) Horn of Africa - Borders reflect Italian, British, French, and Ethiopian dynamics. - Some boundaries are contested or were contested (Eritrea–Ethiopia). - Coastal and desert segments can be geometric; highlands add complexity. 6) Southern Africa - A mix: colonial settler states, protectorates, and later apartheid-era influences. - Borders can be relatively well-demarcated; some follow rivers (Orange River in parts) and some reflect political arrangements (Lesotho, Eswatini). - Namibia’s shape reflects German colonial history and later South African administration. If you want, pick one subregion (e.g., West Africa Sahel, Great Lakes, Horn) and we can go into specific borders and the treaties behind them.
Let’s do West Africa in detail. Why do Ghana, Togo, Benin look like that, and how did those narrow coastal states emerge?

The “narrow coastal states” pattern in parts of West Africa reflects the way European powers established coastal footholds first and then expanded inland, often creating elongated colonies whose inland extent was negotiated later. Ghana (Gold Coast): - British colony formed from coastal forts and inland expansion, including the Ashanti region. - Its eastern border with Togo reflects the partition of German Togoland after WWI: British Togoland was integrated into the Gold Coast (later Ghana) after a UN-supervised plebiscite process, while French Togoland became Togo. Togo: - German Togoland was a relatively narrow strip stretching inland. After WWI it was divided into British and French mandates. - The modern state of Togo is largely the former French Togoland. Its elongated shape is a direct inheritance of that colonial geometry and the coastal partition among European powers. Benin (formerly Dahomey): - French colony with a coastal base and inland reach negotiated with Britain (Nigeria) and Germany (Togoland) at times. - The narrowness relates to the competition for coastal access and the “hinterland” principle—each coastal colony claimed an inland zone behind it, but those inland extents were constrained by neighboring powers’ claims. In general, the West African coast was dotted with forts and trading posts from multiple European states. When the Scramble formalized colonies, those coastal slices became the basis for inland claims, producing vertical, narrow shapes in some places.
How did Nigeria’s borders end up so large and not narrow like Togo?

Nigeria’s size reflects British consolidation of multiple regions and the scale of British influence in that part of West Africa. Key factors: - Britain controlled major coastal access points (Lagos) and the Niger Delta, then expanded influence up the Niger and Benue rivers. - The Niger River system provided a strategic corridor into the interior—commercially and militarily—supporting a large territorial claim. - Britain merged the Northern and Southern Nigeria protectorates in 1914 for administrative reasons, creating a large colony with substantial inland reach. - Boundaries with French territories (Niger, Benin, Cameroon in parts) were negotiated as part of broader Anglo-French arrangements, often with geometric segments in less settled zones. So Nigeria is large partly because Britain had strong riverine access and was willing/able to project control inland, and partly because neighboring French colonies were also vast, leading to a partition that produced big units rather than many small ones.
What about The Gambia? That one is super weird—like a river corridor.

The Gambia is a classic example of a colonial river corridor turning into a state. - Britain established control along the Gambia River for trade and navigation inland. - France controlled the surrounding territory (Senegal). - The colony’s shape reflects a compromise: Britain retained a strip of territory along the river (often described historically as a certain distance from the riverbanks), while France consolidated the surrounding region. Because the Gambia River was the key asset, the boundary essentially wraps around the navigable corridor. It looks “unnatural” if you expect states to be compact, but it’s quite “natural” if you think in terms of controlling a river trade route.
Are there technical definitions like “X miles from the river” that defined The Gambia’s border?

Historically, yes—there were notions of a corridor of influence along the riverbanks, though the exact legal history involves multiple agreements and evolving definitions. The idea was that British control would extend a certain distance north and south of the river, creating a strip. Over time, the boundary was clarified and demarcated more precisely, and the modern border is defined by surveyed lines rather than a simple offset-from-river rule. But the corridor concept is a good mental model for why it looks like it does.
In West Africa, are there borders that follow the Niger River? And does that cause disputes?

Yes, the Niger River serves as a boundary in some stretches, though in many places it runs within a state rather than along a border. Where it forms or influences boundaries: - Parts of the border between Benin and Niger involve the Niger River area. - The Niger–Nigeria boundary is influenced by rivers and colonial agreements, though it’s not simply “the Niger River” as a single boundary line for long stretches. Disputes can arise over: - islands, - fishing rights, - navigation, - and local jurisdiction. But compared to some other regions, many West African states have emphasized cooperative river basin management (e.g., Niger Basin Authority), which can reduce the salience of the border as a conflict trigger, even if local issues persist.
You keep bringing up cooperation institutions. Are those common in Africa for border rivers?

They exist and are significant, though effectiveness varies. Examples: - Senegal River Basin Development Organization (OMVS) involves Senegal, Mauritania, Mali, and Guinea, focusing on dams, navigation, and water management. - Niger Basin Authority covers multiple countries sharing the Niger basin. - Lake Chad Basin Commission covers countries around Lake Chad. These bodies are about shared resources rather than borders per se, but they indirectly support border stability by creating forums for negotiation and joint projects.
Can you contrast this with Europe’s river commissions historically?

Europe has a long history of river commissions tied to navigation and commerce, which sometimes intersected with boundary issues. Examples: - The Central Commission for Navigation on the Rhine (founded in the 19th century) is one of the oldest international organizations, focused on free navigation and harmonized rules. - The Danube has had various international regimes since the 19th century. These institutions emerged in a context of industrialization, dense trade networks, and multiple states sharing key waterways. In Africa, similar logic applies, but the colonial and postcolonial economic structures and state capacities differ, affecting how robust and integrated such regimes become.
Let’s shift to Central Africa. The DRC is huge and its borders look kind of irregular in some places and straight in others. What’s going on there?

The DRC’s borders reflect a mix of river basins, colonial diplomacy, and strategic access. Key influences: 1) Congo River basin logic - The Congo River and its tributaries were central to colonial control and commerce. Some boundaries relate to watershed or river access considerations. 2) International recognition of the Congo Free State - The Congo Free State (later Belgian Congo) was recognized through international diplomacy, and its boundaries were negotiated with neighboring colonial powers (France, Britain, Portugal, Germany). - Those negotiations produced a mix of natural-feature and geometric segments. 3) Access corridors - The DRC’s narrow access to the Atlantic (around the Congo River mouth) reflects the desire to ensure the Congo polity had an outlet to the sea, while Portugal retained Angola to the south and France controlled territory to the north (Congo-Brazzaville, Gabon). 4) Great Lakes region complexity - Eastern borders near the Great Lakes are influenced by lakes, mountains, and dense settlement, producing more intricate boundaries and later disputes. So the DRC’s shape is a product of being a massive river-basin-centered colony whose edges were carved by multiple competing empires and later stabilized.
The DRC’s Atlantic coastline is tiny. Was that intentional?

It was shaped by competing colonial claims. Portugal’s Angola and France’s interests north of the Congo limited how much coastline the Congo polity could have. Ensuring some outlet to the sea was important for trade and legitimacy, but it didn’t require a broad coastline—control of the Congo River mouth region was the key. So “intentional” in the sense of negotiated compromise: yes, the result reflects bargaining and the balance of power among colonial states.
East Africa now—Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda. Their borders feel less geometric in some places. Is that because of lakes?

Lakes are a major part of it, along with different colonial histories and denser populations in some zones. - Lake Victoria creates water boundaries among Uganda, Kenya, and Tanzania. Delimiting lake boundaries involves median lines, islands, and sometimes colonial-era administrative lines. - Lake Tanganyika is a major boundary between Tanzania and the DRC, and between Tanzania and Burundi, with complex maritime-style delimitation issues on a lake. - Colonial territories: German East Africa (later Tanganyika under British mandate) vs British Kenya and Uganda influenced how lines were drawn. Also, the East African Rift and associated highlands create more opportunities for boundaries to follow ridges or to be influenced by terrain, though politics still dominates.
Are lake boundaries simpler than rivers, since lakes don’t move as much?

They can be simpler in some ways because you don’t have a shifting channel, but they have their own complexities: - How to draw the boundary: equidistance/median lines are common, but islands complicate them. - Water level changes can alter shorelines; if the boundary is defined by the shoreline, changes matter. If it’s defined by coordinates or a median line based on a reference shoreline, it’s more stable. - Fishing rights and resource exploitation can raise disputes. So lakes reduce the meander problem but introduce delimitation geometry and resource governance issues.
Can you give an example of an island dispute in a lake or river in Africa?

Besides Kasikili/Sedudu (river island), there have been disputes around Lake Victoria islands historically, notably Migingo Island (Kenya–Uganda). That dispute involved competing claims and enforcement actions tied to fishing rights. While not purely about the island’s size, it highlighted how small features can become significant when economic stakes (like fisheries) are high and the precise boundary is unclear or contested.
How do countries actually resolve these disputes—do they always go to the ICJ?

Not always. Options include: 1) Bilateral negotiation - The most common approach, sometimes with joint boundary commissions. 2) Mediation or regional organizations - African Union or regional bodies can facilitate dialogue. 3) Arbitration - States can agree to an arbitral tribunal with specific terms. 4) International courts - ICJ (International Court of Justice) for disputes between states that consent to its jurisdiction. - ITLOS for maritime issues, sometimes special chambers. Courts are used when negotiations stall or when both sides want a definitive legal resolution. But many states prefer negotiated settlements because they can be more flexible and less politically risky than an all-or-nothing judgment.
Does the straightness of borders correlate with conflict? People often imply it does.

It’s not a simple correlation. - Straight borders can cut across communities and ecological systems, which can contribute to tensions, especially where governance is weak. - But conflict depends on many factors: state capacity, economic conditions, external вмешательство, resources, historical grievances, and political incentives. - Some straight borders are peaceful (e.g., U.S.–Canada largely). - Some irregular borders have been conflict zones too (Balkans, Caucasus). So border geometry can be a contributing factor in certain contexts, but it’s not a deterministic predictor of conflict.
If straight borders aren’t inherently conflict-prone, what makes them problematic in some African contexts?

The problem often isn’t the straightness itself but what it signifies and what it intersects: - It may reflect external delimitation that didn’t account for local land use (pastoral routes, seasonal water points). - It can split cohesive economic and cultural regions, creating cross-border kinship networks that states may securitize. - In remote regions, a straight line may be poorly demarcated and lightly governed, creating space for smuggling or armed groups. - If resources are discovered near the line, uncertainty or competing interpretations can escalate. In other words, geometry is a proxy for a broader historical and administrative story.
How do modern mapping technologies—GPS, satellite imagery—change border management?

They change it a lot: 1) Better demarcation - GPS allows precise placement and recording of boundary markers. - Satellite imagery helps identify terrain features, settlements, and river channels. 2) Reduced ambiguity - Coordinate lists can be updated in consistent datums (e.g., WGS84), reducing old datum confusion. 3) New disputes or visibility - Improved mapping can reveal that communities or infrastructure lie on the “other” side of a previously vague line, prompting political friction. - It can also expose inconsistencies between old maps and on-the-ground realities. 4) Borderland development - Better maps support joint planning: roads, customs posts, conservation areas. So technology can both resolve and surface issues, depending on political willingness to cooperate.
Does datum choice really matter that much?

It can. Different datums represent different best-fit ellipsoids and reference points for Earth. A coordinate (latitude/longitude) in one datum can correspond to a point tens or even hundreds of meters away in another datum, depending on location. For a border: - If the treaty uses coordinates but doesn’t specify datum, modern re-interpretation can shift the line. - If boundary pillars were placed using an older local datum, converting them to WGS84 requires careful transformation. In high-stakes or densely populated borderlands, even small shifts matter.
Okay, last big angle: how do internal administrative boundaries relate to international borders in Africa? Are they similarly geometric?

Often, yes—internal boundaries can also be geometric, especially where colonial administrations drew districts for convenience. Post-independence governments have also created new provinces/regions for governance, sometimes using straight lines for simplicity. But internal boundaries can be changed more easily than international ones, so they may evolve to reflect: - population distribution, - ethnic or linguistic considerations (sometimes), - administrative workload, - political balancing. Still, the legacy of colonial cartography can persist at multiple levels of governance.
Can you summarize the key misconceptions people have when looking at Africa’s straight borders?

Common misconceptions include: 1) “Berlin Conference drew all borders with rulers.” Reality: Berlin set rules; many borders came from later bilateral treaties and commissions. 2) “African borders ignore all natural features.” Reality: Many follow rivers, lakes, and sometimes ridges; it’s a mixed pattern. 3) “Europe’s borders are natural and peaceful because of geography.” Reality: Europe has many historically contingent borders; peace is a modern political achievement more than a natural outcome. 4) “Straight borders automatically cause conflict.” Reality: Conflict depends on governance, resources, history, and politics; geometry is only one factor. 5) “Straight borders are easy to demarcate.” Reality: Surveying a parallel/meridian in remote terrain can be very difficult, and early errors persist.
I want to go deeper on one technical point: when a treaty says “the boundary follows the 10th parallel,” is that a geodesic line or something else?

A parallel is a line of constant latitude. On the Earth (approximated as an ellipsoid), it’s not a geodesic except for the equator. A geodesic is the shortest path between two points; parallels (other than the equator) are “small circles,” not shortest paths. So “the 10th parallel” means: - every point on the boundary has latitude 10°N (in the specified datum/ellipsoid, ideally). - It’s conceptually precise, but demarcation requires determining latitude on the ground. If a treaty instead says “a straight line between point A and point B,” that could be interpreted as a geodesic—unless the treaty context implies a line drawn on a specific projection.
And for meridians?

Meridians (lines of constant longitude) are geodesics on a sphere and are close to geodesics on an ellipsoid in the sense that they represent north-south lines connecting the poles. Practically, “the 20th meridian east” is a clear geometric concept, but again it depends on the chosen prime meridian reference (now Greenwich, historically sometimes others) and the datum.
Were there cases where treaties referenced a different prime meridian?

Historically, yes—before global standardization, some maps used different prime meridians (Paris, Ferro, etc.). By the late 19th century, Greenwich became dominant, especially after the 1884 International Meridian Conference, but older documents can have mixed references. In colonial boundary treaties, the prime meridian was usually implicit in the mapping conventions of the drafting powers, but ambiguity can arise when later interpreters try to reconcile documents from different sources. Boundary commissions often had to harmonize these references.
If you had to recommend how to teach this topic without falling into stereotypes, what would you emphasize?

I’d emphasize: - Process over appearance: explain how borders are made (diplomacy, administration, surveying, law) rather than implying a moral judgment from geometry alone. - Comparative examples: show that straight borders exist in many places (U.S.–Canada, Australia) and that “natural” borders can be just as political. - Multiple layers: distinguish between delimitation and demarcation; show how maps, treaties, and on-the-ground administration interact. - Local consequences without determinism: discuss how borders intersect with trade, mobility, and governance, but avoid implying borders alone “cause” outcomes. - Specific case studies: Egypt–Sudan, Kasikili/Sedudu, Aouzou, Togoland partition—so students see concrete mechanisms.
Can we do one more detailed case study: the partition of Togoland and how that shaped Ghana and Togo?

Yes. 1) German Togoland (pre-WWI) - Germany established Togoland as a colony along the Gulf of Guinea, relatively narrow but extending inland. - Its borders were shaped by negotiations with Britain (Gold Coast) and France (Dahomey) and reflected coastal partition and inland spheres of influence. 2) WWI and League of Nations mandates - After Germany’s defeat, Togoland was divided between Britain and France under League of Nations mandates. - British Togoland was administered alongside the Gold Coast; French Togoland was administered separately. 3) UN trusteeship and plebiscite - Post-WWII, the mandates became UN trust territories. - A plebiscite process occurred in British Togoland (1956) where the population voted on integration with the Gold Coast or other options; integration won in the main. - When the Gold Coast became independent as Ghana (1957), it included the former British Togoland. - French Togoland became the independent Republic of Togo (1960). 4) Border implications - The Ghana–Togo border thus reflects the division of a former colony by mandate administration, later translated into international boundaries through decolonization processes. - This is a case where borders were shaped not only by 19th-century colonial diplomacy but also by 20th-century international governance mechanisms (League/UN trusteeship). So the “shape” of Togo is not just a random ruler line; it’s the legacy of a specific colonial entity and its postwar partition.
Thanks. One last question: if Europe had been partitioned by external powers in the 1880s with limited mapping, would its borders look more like Africa’s?

Likely more geometric in the less-mapped or less-negotiated interiors, yes. When borders are drawn quickly to separate spheres of influence, especially by external actors using small-scale maps, straight segments are a common outcome—regardless of continent. But Europe in the 1880s already had dense settlement, strong states, detailed cadastral mapping in many areas, and entrenched political claims. Those conditions force more detailed, locally negotiated borders (and make purely geometric lines harder to impose without provoking immediate resistance). So the key difference is not continental destiny; it’s the combination of power, knowledge, administrative capacity, and the political context of boundary-making.
Can you keep going with more examples of straight borders outside Africa to reinforce that point?

Sure—here are several prominent non-African straight or geometric borders and why they exist: 1) U.S.–Canada (49th parallel and others) - Large stretches follow the 49th parallel, established by treaties (1818, 1846 Oregon Treaty). - It reflects a diplomatic compromise to avoid conflict and simplify division across vast territories. 2) U.S. internal state borders - Many western state boundaries are straight lines (Colorado, Wyoming) based on parallels and meridians—administrative convenience during territorial organization. 3) Middle East (some segments) - Several borders have long straight segments tied to early 20th-century imperial agreements and mandates, negotiated with limited local participation. 4) Australia’s state/territory borders - Many follow meridians/parallels (Western Australia’s eastern border at 129°E, etc.), reflecting colonial administrative decisions. 5) Southern South America (some segments) - While the Andes dominate many boundaries, there are also straight segments in less populated areas resulting from treaty simplification. 6) Antarctica sector claims (where asserted) - Not universally recognized, but claims are often defined by meridians converging at the pole—pure geometry. These examples show that geometric borders are a general statecraft tool. Africa stands out because many of its international borders were finalized in a relatively short colonial period and then preserved into the modern era.
That answers my question thoroughly. I’d like you to continue a bit more on how maps themselves—like projection and scale—shape public perceptions of border “straightness.”

Public perception is strongly shaped by cartographic choices: 1) Projection effects - Mercator and other common projections preserve angles but distort area and shape, especially toward the poles. While Africa is less distorted than high-latitude regions, the projection still affects how “straight” a line looks relative to geographic reality. - Lines of latitude and longitude appear as straight grid lines on many projections, making borders based on them look especially ruler-like. 2) Scale and generalization - Small-scale world maps simplify borders. Minor deviations and local kinks disappear, making borders look straighter. - At larger scales (regional maps), you may see the actual demarcation path or river meanders, which changes the impression. 3) Line styling - Thick border strokes in political maps emphasize the boundary as a hard line. Thicker lines visually “straighten” features by hiding small wiggles. 4) Selection bias in what gets taught - Textbooks often highlight dramatic examples (Sahara lines, 49th parallel) because they’re easy to see and remember. - More complex borders require more explanation, so they’re less commonly used as quick illustrations. 5) The grid illusion - When a border aligns with a graticule (lat/long grid), it feels “artificial” because it matches the map’s coordinate scaffolding. That’s a psychological effect: viewers notice alignment with the map’s geometry. So maps don’t just depict borders; they shape how borders are interpreted and remembered.
Why is Manhattan a grid but London is a maze? What determines a city's street layout?

Street layout is mostly a fossil record of how a city grew, plus the planning tools and goals that were available at the time. Broadly: - Grids tend to appear when land is subdivided deliberately (often quickly), when surveying is standardized, and when authorities want predictable parcels, taxation, and circulation. They’re also easier to extend. - “Organic” or “maze-like” patterns often come from incremental growth over centuries—paths become lanes, lanes become streets—shaped by property boundaries, topography, old walls, rivers, and political fragmentation. Manhattan’s grid is famously tied to the 1811 Commissioners’ Plan: a deliberate, large-scale imposition of a regular street network to facilitate real estate development and expansion. London’s core street pattern predates modern planning: Roman roads, medieval lanes, parish boundaries, former fields, and later layers of rebuilding after fires and wars. Even where London has planned pieces (like parts of the West End estates), they’re stitched into a much older fabric. If you want a technical framework, it’s usually a mix of: 1) Era of major growth (pre- vs post-industrial surveying and governance) 2) Land ownership and subdivision mechanisms 3) Topography and water systems 4) Defense and fortifications (walls, gates) 5) Transport technology changes (walk/horse vs tram/rail vs car) 6) Planning ideology (order, hygiene, aesthetics, control, speculation)
So is it mostly “planned vs unplanned”? Or are there cases where grids happen organically too?

“Planned vs unplanned” is a helpful shorthand, but it’s not binary. Grids can be: - Planned from scratch (Manhattan 1811; many colonial towns; Spanish colonial “Laws of the Indies”; many US western towns under the Public Land Survey System). - Introduced as a corrective layer over time (a city adds a grid in new suburbs while the old core remains irregular). - Emergent from repeated subdivision patterns: if landowners repeatedly parcel fields in roughly orthogonal strips, the resulting lanes can approximate a grid even without a single master plan. It’s less common to get a clean, citywide grid “organically,” but you can get grid-like regularity locally. Organic patterns can also be “planned,” just not in a rectilinear way: - Baroque planning uses radial avenues, vistas, and diagonals (think Paris’ later interventions, or Washington, DC’s diagonal avenues over a grid). - Garden city and curvilinear suburban planning is very intentional but looks “organic” because it follows contours and emphasizes loops/cul-de-sacs. So the more precise distinction is: centralized, large-area subdivision under a single framework vs incremental, multi-owner accretion with many small decisions.
What are the main advantages of a grid, from an urban planning perspective?

Advantages depend on what you optimize for, but classic grid benefits include: 1) Legibility and navigation - Repetition and orthogonality make it easier to orient, address, and wayfind. - Numbered streets/avenues can encode location. 2) Parcelization and real estate - Regular blocks simplify surveying, lot creation, and property transfer. - Predictable frontage and block dimensions make development and valuation straightforward. 3) Network connectivity and redundancy - Many route choices distribute traffic (in theory), offering resilience if one street is blocked. - Permeability supports walking and biking by providing multiple direct paths. 4) Infrastructure efficiency - Utilities (water, sewer, power, telecom) can be laid in a regular pattern; maintenance access is simplified. - Service delivery (trash, emergency response) is more routinizable. 5) Expandability - A grid can be extended outward without rethinking geometry each time (though topography can complicate this). But grids also have tradeoffs: - Long straight streets can encourage higher vehicle speeds unless traffic-calmed. - Monotony and lack of focal points can be a downside unless landmarks, parks, or diagonals break it up. - A rigid grid may ignore topography, causing steep streets, drainage problems, or costly grading (San Francisco is the classic “grid meets hills” case).
And what are the advantages of an organic street pattern? Besides “it’s charming.”

Beyond aesthetics, organic patterns can have functional advantages: 1) Topographic fit and drainage - Streets that follow contours reduce grading, manage slopes, and can align with natural drainage. - Curving alignments can avoid floodplains or unstable ground. 2) Traffic calming by geometry - Irregular, narrow, and winding streets naturally slow vehicles, which can improve safety in residential areas. - Fewer long sightlines can reduce speeding (though they can also reduce visibility at intersections). 3) Microclimate and comfort - Street orientation affects wind, sun exposure, and shading. Organic patterns sometimes create more varied microclimates and sheltered spaces. 4) Incremental adaptability - Because it evolves in pieces, an organic network can adapt to small constraints—existing buildings, parcels, rights-of-way—without requiring massive clearance. 5) Place-making through variety - Irregular nodes can produce distinctive squares, junctions, and “rooms” in the city that become social centers. Tradeoffs: - Wayfinding can be harder. - Irregular parcels can complicate development and infrastructure. - Connectivity can be poor if the pattern becomes dendritic (lots of cul-de-sacs), which is “organic-looking” but not organically grown in the historic sense.
You mentioned surveying systems. How much did technology—like accurate maps—change street patterns?

A lot. Surveying and mapping are enabling technologies for certain geometries. Key shifts: - Standardized surveying instruments and methods allowed consistent angles and distances over large areas. Once you can reliably set right angles and measure long baselines, large grids become practical. - Cadastres (systematic land parcel records) made it easier to tax and transact property, which incentivized regular parcels. - National or imperial mapping projects improved coordination between jurisdictions and reduced disputes. In places where: - Authority is fragmented (many landowners, parishes, guilds), - Records are inconsistent, - And streets emerge as customary rights-of-way, you tend to get more irregularity, because each segment is negotiated locally. A technical detail: even with good tools, implementing a citywide grid requires political power—someone has to impose it, acquire rights-of-way, and sometimes override existing property lines. Mapping is necessary but not sufficient.
That makes sense. But London had the Great Fire. Why didn’t they just rebuild it as a grid then?

After the Great Fire of 1666, there were proposals for more regular plans—some with grid-like elements and grand boulevards. The main reasons London didn’t become a clean-slate grid include: 1) Property rights and speed of rebuilding - The priority was rapid reconstruction. Re-parceling land into a new geometry would have required renegotiating thousands of property claims. - Compensation and legal disputes would have delayed rebuilding for years, possibly decades. 2) Governance fragmentation - Multiple authorities and stakeholders: the Crown, the City of London, parishes, livery companies, private landowners. - Coordinating a single comprehensive plan was politically difficult. 3) Infrastructure and commerce - London was a working port and commercial hub. Prolonged disruption was costly. - Rebuilding “where it was” allowed businesses to resume. 4) Path dependence - Even if you straighten a few streets, the underlying medieval parcel pattern often persists, pulling the network back into irregularity. What did change: building regulations (materials, street widths in some places), some street improvements, and later planned estates in the West End. So London got layered regularity in pockets rather than a wholesale reset.
Are there examples of cities that did get rebuilt into a grid after a disaster?

Yes. Disasters sometimes create political windows for re-planning—if land can be reassembled and authority is centralized. Examples: - Lisbon after the 1755 earthquake: the Baixa Pombalina was rebuilt with a more regular, rational street plan and standardized building types under the Marquis of Pombal. - Parts of Chicago after the Great Fire of 1871 weren’t “re-gridded” because Chicago already had a grid, but rebuilding accelerated modernization and building codes. - Some Japanese cities after WWII reconstruction had major re-planning through land readjustment (not always a strict grid, but more ordered). - Earthquake rebuilds in various places have introduced straighter streets and wider rights-of-way for firebreaks and evacuation. The key mechanism is often land readjustment (pooling parcels, re-plotting them, and returning smaller-but-more-valuable plots), which makes reconfiguration feasible without outright expropriation.
How do topography and water shape grids? Like, can a grid “bend” around hills or rivers?

Definitely. Real grids are often “softened” by constraints. Common adaptations: - Grid rotation: a city may have multiple grids at different orientations, each aligned to a shoreline, prevailing winds, or a survey baseline. Where they meet, you get skewed intersections and odd parcels (classic in many cities). - Interrupted grids: rivers, rail corridors, parks, or steep ravines break continuity; bridges and underpasses become key connectors. - Terraced or stepped grids: on slopes, blocks might remain rectilinear in plan but require stair streets, switchbacks, or retaining structures in section. - Curved “grid” on a coastline: streets remain roughly parallel/perpendicular locally, but the whole system arcs to follow the shore. Water specifically: - Historic shorelines and filled land create discontinuities: a grid laid on reclaimed land can be very regular, while the older inland fabric is irregular. - Drainage: rigid grids can fight natural drainage lines, requiring engineered sewers and culverts; organic alignments sometimes coincide with natural channels (though many old cities also built over streams). So “grid vs organic” is often more like: what’s the dominant ordering principle, and how often does it yield to constraints?
What about climate? Do planners orient grids for sun and wind?

Sometimes, yes—especially in planned settlements where climate comfort mattered and there was enough control to implement orientation. Sun: - In hot climates, narrow streets and building height-to-width ratios can create shade. Orientation can minimize direct solar exposure at peak times. - In colder climates, orientation can maximize winter sun on streets and façades, though other factors (wind, snow drifting) matter too. Wind: - Aligning long straight streets with prevailing winds can create wind tunnels—bad in cold/windy climates, sometimes useful for ventilation in hot/humid ones. - Some plans avoid continuous wind corridors by introducing offsets, curves, or varied block sizes. But historically, many grids were oriented to: - Survey baselines, - Shorelines or rivers, - Political boundaries, - Or symbolic axes, rather than microclimate optimization. Modern planning and urban design pay more attention to solar access and wind at the district scale, sometimes with computational modeling.
You mentioned the Spanish colonial laws. Were those grids different from Manhattan’s?

Yes, the Spanish colonial grid (often associated with the Laws of the Indies, 16th century) has a distinctive logic: - Central plaza: The grid is organized around a main square (plaza mayor) as the civic and religious heart, with key institutions placed on it. - Hierarchy: Streets radiate from a central order, and blocks can be subdivided in standardized ways. - Orientation: Often tuned to climate (at least in principle) and to practical siting (water, defensibility), though implementation varied. - Governance: It was a tool of administration and colonization—imposing a recognizable, controllable urban form. Manhattan’s grid: - Is more uniformly repetitive over a large area. - Lacks a single central plaza organizing the whole (Central Park arrives later; there are squares and parks but not one dominant plaza). - Is heavily tied to real estate commodification: regular lots, predictable development. Both are “grids,” but the social and spatial intentions differ.
Is the grid inherently better for transit? Like buses, trams, subways?

For surface transit (buses, trams), grids can be very good because: - Straight corridors simplify routing and reduce travel time. - Regular spacing supports frequent service and easy transfers (grid transit networks are a whole planning concept). - Many parallel streets give flexibility for dedicated lanes or diversions. But it’s not automatic. What matters is: - Right-of-way width: narrow historic streets can’t accommodate dedicated lanes or platforms easily. - Intersection spacing and signal priority: too many intersections can slow transit unless prioritized. - Demand patterns: if destinations are concentrated in a few corridors, a radial network may be more efficient than a pure grid. For subways/metros: - Underground lines can be independent of street geometry, though construction often follows streets to reduce property acquisition and utility conflicts. - In older cities with organic patterns, metro lines may cut across at efficient angles, creating a network that doesn’t mirror the street pattern. So grids are “compatible” with transit, but the actual performance depends on street design and operational policy.
What about emergency response? I’ve heard grids are better for fire trucks and ambulances.
