A) Extent: Global maps 1) If you must compare areas (thematic like land cover, climate zone extent, deforestation): - Choose equal-area world projections: - Equal Earth (modern, good aesthetics) - Mollweide (classic) - Eckert IV / VI (various compromises within equal-area) - Interrupted equal-area (Goode) if land-focused and you can accept cuts 2) If you must preserve directions from a central point (azimuths), e.g., radio propagation from a transmitter, earthquake wavefronts from epicenter: - Azimuthal projections centered on the point: - Azimuthal equidistant (distance true from center) - Lambert azimuthal equal-area (area true, good for hemispheres) 3) If you’re drawing great-circle paths and want them as straight lines: - Gnomonic (great circles are straight), but distortion becomes extreme away from center—usually for specialized use. 4) If you want a general reference world map: - Compromise projections: - Winkel Tripel - Robinson - Natural Earth (not to be confused with Equal Earth) B) Extent: Continental / regional 1) Mid-latitude regions (e.g., a continent spanning east-west): - Conformal for shape/navigation: - Lambert conformal conic (common for aeronautical charts, weather maps) - Equal-area for thematic: - Albers equal-area conic 2) Regions spanning north-south: - Transverse Mercator (conformal) for long narrow N–S zones - Lambert azimuthal equal-area for broad regions around a center C) Extent: Local / city-scale - Distortion is small if you pick a suitable local projection. - Conformal is often preferred for engineering and cadastral: - Transverse Mercator / UTM zone - Local state plane systems - For area-sensitive local planning: - Local equal-area variants exist, but often conformal is fine because area distortion is tiny at city scale. D) Special thematic constraints - If your map is a choropleth: lean equal-area. - If your map is a flow map with bearings: consider conformal or an azimuthal setup. - If your map is distance-based analysis: equidistant projections can be valuable, but note “equidistant” usually means distances are true from one point or along certain lines, not everywhere. So the “tree” is: decide what to preserve (area/angle/distance/direction), decide extent (global/regional/local), then choose a family (cylindrical/conic/azimuthal/compromise) that matches geometry.
Can you explain why conic projections are so good for mid-latitudes? It feels like magic that they fit the shape of a continent.

It’s geometry matching geometry. A conic projection conceptually wraps a cone around the globe, then unrolls it. Where the cone touches (tangent) or cuts through (secant) the globe are the standard parallel(s)—latitudes where distortion is minimized. Why this suits mid-latitudes: - Many large inhabited landmasses in mid-latitudes extend more east-west than north-south. - Parallels (lines of latitude) are roughly horizontal bands; conic projections make parallels arcs of circles and meridians radiating lines, which often aligns well with the way such regions “spread.” Distortion behavior: - In a secant conic projection (two standard parallels), scale is exact along those two latitudes and small between them. - Distortion increases as you move away north or south of those standard parallels. So if you choose standard parallels to bracket your region, you “trap” the area of interest in the low-distortion zone. That’s why Albers equal-area conic is a favorite for thematic maps of large mid-latitude countries/continents, and Lambert conformal conic is a favorite when preserving shape/angles matters.
Let’s do a weird one: why do polar maps often use azimuthal projections? And what distortions should I expect there?

Polar regions are naturally suited to azimuthal projections because azimuthal projections are defined by projecting the globe onto a plane. If you place that plane tangent to the Earth at a pole, you get a polar aspect: the pole is the center of the map. Why it’s a good fit: - The pole is a natural “center” for polar phenomena. - Meridians radiate out like spokes, matching the reality that all longitudes meet at the pole. - Parallels become concentric circles—very intuitive for polar geometry. Common polar azimuthal choices: - Stereographic (conformal): preserves angles, used for navigation and meteorology in polar regions. - Lambert azimuthal equal-area: preserves area, good for thematic polar maps. - Azimuthal equidistant: distances from the pole are true, useful for range rings. Expected distortions: - Distortion is minimal at the center (the pole) and increases with distance from it. - You typically sacrifice accuracy near the “rim” (the edge of the map), which might be at some chosen latitude like 60°. - Depending on the specific azimuthal projection, you’ll preserve either angles, areas, or distances from center, but not all. So polar azimuthals are like “the projection’s sweet spot matches the region’s geometry.”
I’m curious about one more thing: why do some projections have “cusps” or weird shapes at the edges? Like the heart-shaped ones.

Those are often pseudocylindrical or other specialized projections designed to optimize a certain distortion pattern or aesthetic, and the “weird edge” is a byproduct of how the graticule (grid of meridians/parallels) maps. For example: - Bonne projection can have a heart-like outline depending on parameters. - Some equal-area projections produce lobed or cusp-like boundaries because they compress the poles to points or lines and shape the boundary to maintain area. Why edges get weird: - A projection is a transformation from a closed surface (sphere/ellipsoid) to a plane. If you want to keep certain properties (equal-area, or low distortion over most of the map), the “cost” can be pushed to the boundary: extreme stretching/compression, or a boundary that is not a simple rectangle. - Many projections choose a boundary that corresponds to some meridian being “cut” (like ±180°) and some latitude being mapped to an edge. The shape of that edge depends on the math. So the boundary is not arbitrary decoration—it’s where the projection’s compromises become visible.
I want a deeper intuitive explanation of why no projection can preserve everything. Don’t just say “it’s impossible.” Give me the geometric reason.

The geometric reason is curvature. A sphere (or ellipsoid) has intrinsic curvature: if you live on its surface and measure triangles, circles, and angles, the geometry is non-Euclidean. A plane is flat: Euclidean geometry. A few concrete manifestations: 1) Angle sum of triangles On a plane, the angles of a triangle sum to 180°. On a sphere, the sum is >180° for sufficiently large triangles (spherical excess). That’s an intrinsic property—you can measure it without referencing 3D space. If a map preserved all distances (an isometry), it would preserve triangle geometry, including angle sums. But you can’t have spherical triangles behaving like planar triangles everywhere. 2) Gauss’s Theorema Egregium (the big one) Gaussian curvature is intrinsic and preserved under bending without stretching. You can roll a plane into a cylinder without stretching (curvature stays zero intrinsically), but you cannot bend a sphere into a plane without stretching or tearing because the sphere’s curvature is positive. Map projections are essentially “flattening” operations. Flattening a curved surface forces either stretching (distortion) or cutting (interruptions). 3) Local scale cannot be 1 everywhere If you tried to keep scale true everywhere, you’d be preserving distances locally in all directions, which implies preserving curvature relations. That’s incompatible between sphere and plane. So the reason isn’t “cartographers didn’t find the right formula.” It’s that the globe and the plane have fundamentally different intrinsic geometry. You can preserve one class of properties (angles, areas, certain distances) by allowing others to distort, but you can’t preserve all simultaneously across the whole surface.
Okay. Now I want to do a “projection tasting menu.” Give me like 12 projections, each with a vibe, what it’s best for, and the signature distortion. Make it vivid.

1) Mercator (conformal cylindrical) Vibe: “Everything is upright and navigable.” Best for: local shape fidelity, rhumb lines as straight lines, web basemaps (via Web Mercator). Signature distortion: area explodes toward the poles; poles unreachable. 2) Web Mercator (pseudo-Mercator) Vibe: “Infinite scroll map universe.” Best for: tiled web mapping, interactive zooming, street maps. Signature distortion: same as Mercator + clamped polar caps; area bias baked into the internet. 3) Mollweide (equal-area) Vibe: “Truth in area, oval world.” Best for: global thematic maps comparing extents. Signature distortion: shapes get squashed/stretch near edges; angles off. 4) Equal Earth (equal-area) Vibe: “Modern equal-area that doesn’t look alien.” Best for: global thematic/reference where area honesty matters. Signature distortion: moderate shape changes, but designed to keep continents recognizable. 5) Gall–Peters (equal-area cylindrical) Vibe: “Tall tropics, squashed high latitudes.” Best for: area comparisons in a cylindrical frame; provocative educational maps. Signature distortion: severe shape distortion; equatorial regions look stretched vertically. 6) Robinson (compromise) Vibe: “Classic classroom wall map.” Best for: general reference. Signature distortion: nothing is perfect; distortion spread smoothly. 7) Winkel Tripel (compromise) Vibe: “Balanced and modern reference.” Best for: general world maps with low overall distortion. Signature distortion: moderate, diffuse; edges still distort. 8) Lambert conformal conic (conformal) Vibe: “Mid-latitude precision.” Best for: aeronautical charts, weather maps, regions wider than tall. Signature distortion: area varies; scale increases away from standard parallels. 9) Albers equal-area conic (equal-area) Vibe: “Thematic powerhouse for continents.” Best for: choropleths and area-based thematic maps in mid-latitudes. Signature distortion: shapes not perfect; but area is right. 10) Stereographic (conformal azimuthal) Vibe: “Polar elegance.” Best for: polar navigation, meteorology, any map centered on a point needing angles. Signature distortion: area blows up away from center. 11) Lambert azimuthal equal-area (equal-area azimuthal) Vibe: “Hemispheres with honest area.” Best for: thematic hemispheric maps, continent-centered maps. Signature distortion: shapes distort toward edge, but area preserved. 12) Gnomonic (azimuthal, great circles straight) Vibe: “Geodesic wizardry.” Best for: plotting shortest paths as straight lines (from the center). Signature distortion: extreme away from center; only practical for limited extents. If you tell me what kind of map you’re imagining (global thematic, reference, polar, etc.), I can narrow this into a “best 3” and explain why.
I want to nerd out about graticules. Why do some projections have straight meridians and curved parallels, and vice versa? What determines that?

The graticule shape is the fingerprint of the projection’s geometry and chosen aspect (normal, transverse, oblique). General patterns by family: 1) Cylindrical projections (normal aspect) - Meridians: straight vertical lines, equally spaced in x (often proportional to longitude). - Parallels: straight horizontal lines, spaced according to the projection’s y(φ) formula. Examples: Mercator, equirectangular, cylindrical equal-area. Reason: You’re conceptually projecting onto a cylinder tangent to the equator, then unrolling. 2) Conic projections (normal aspect) - Meridians: straight lines converging at a point (the cone’s apex). - Parallels: arcs of circles centered on the apex. Examples: Lambert conformal conic, Albers. Reason: Unrolled cone produces radial meridians and circular parallels. 3) Azimuthal projections - Meridians: straight lines radiating from the center (in polar aspect). - Parallels: concentric circles. Reason: Projection onto a plane; directions from center map to angles. 4) Pseudocylindrical projections - Parallels: straight horizontal lines. - Central meridian: straight vertical line. - Other meridians: curved. Examples: Mollweide, Sinusoidal, Eckert. Reason: They keep latitude lines simple while shaping meridians to manage area/shape. Aspect flips: If you rotate the projection (transverse or oblique aspect), the “straight line” roles can change. For example, Transverse Mercator makes the central meridian straight, but the equator becomes curved. So what determines it is: - The developable surface concept (cylinder/cone/plane) and - The mathematical mapping functions x(λ,φ), y(λ,φ) and - The chosen aspect (orientation) and central lines you want to be “nice.”
Can you explain the sinusoidal projection? It sounds simple. Why isn’t it more common?

The sinusoidal projection is an equal-area pseudocylindrical projection with a very clean formula: x = R * λ * cos φ y = R * φ Properties: - Equal-area: yes. - Parallels are straight, evenly spaced (because y is proportional to latitude). - The central meridian is straight. - Other meridians are sinusoidal curves (hence the name), because x shrinks with cosφ. Why it’s not the default for reference maps: - Shape distortion increases away from the central meridian, especially near the outer edges. - Continents near the edges look sheared. - It has a “pinched” look at the poles because meridians converge there. Why it is used: - Thematic maps where equal-area is key and you can choose a central meridian that keeps your region of interest near the center. - It’s also used as part of composite/interrupted projections (like Goode homolosine combines sinusoidal and Mollweide to reduce distortion). So: it’s elegant mathematically, but visually it’s less balanced for a general world reference unless you choose interruptions or carefully center it.
Goode homolosine: explain the “splice.” How does combining two projections even work?

Goode’s homolosine is an interrupted equal-area projection that blends two equal-area projections: - Sinusoidal near the equator - Mollweide in higher latitudes The motivation: - Sinusoidal has low distortion near the equator but worse near the poles. - Mollweide handles higher latitudes better (in terms of shape distribution), but can be more distorted near the equator in some ways. The “splice”: - Choose a latitude (around 40°44′) where the two projections have compatible scale characteristics. - Use sinusoidal formulas for |φ| below that latitude. - Use Mollweide formulas above it. - Ensure continuity along the splice latitude (so there isn’t a jump). Because both are equal-area, the resulting composite remains equal-area (with proper matching), but you get lower overall distortion for landmasses—at the cost of interruptions (cuts in the oceans) to keep continents intact. Combining projections is like tailoring fabric: - Different weaves behave better in different parts of the garment. - You stitch them along a seam where the patterns align.
I’m thinking about the psychological side of map perception: why do we trust some projections more than others? Is it just familiarity?

Familiarity is huge, but there are several perceptual cues: 1) Orthogonality and “grid comfort” Projections with straight, perpendicular graticules (like Mercator or equirectangular) look “mathematical” and orderly. People interpret that as correctness, even if it’s misleading in area. 2) Shape recognition bias We trust maps where coastlines look like what we’ve learned. If a projection makes familiar shapes look warped (like Peters), we perceive it as wrong, even if it’s doing something important (equal-area). 3) Centering and framing effects What’s in the center looks primary. Projections that center certain regions can feel more “natural” to viewers used to that layout. 4) Distortion salience Some distortions are more noticeable than others. Area distortion is surprisingly hard to see unless you’re primed for it. Shape distortion is immediately obvious. 5) Rectangular vs oval boundary A rectangular map boundary feels like a “complete” picture and fits pages/screens, so people treat it as standard. Oval boundaries can feel like a special-purpose map, even when distortion is lower. So yes, familiarity, but also: the human visual system is more sensitive to angles and shapes than to area. That’s why conformal maps can “feel” right while being numerically wrong in area.
I want to simulate that bias: give me a thought experiment where someone would confidently make a wrong inference from a Mercator map.

Imagine a choropleth showing “percentage of land covered by forest” by country, with darker meaning higher percentage. On a Mercator basemap: - High-latitude countries appear larger than they are. - If some of them are dark, the viewer’s eye sees a huge dark swath across the top of the map. Wrong inference: “This variable is concentrated mostly in the far north; it dominates the world.” But that inference is partly projection-induced: - The actual land area at those latitudes is much smaller proportionally than it appears. - The viewer is unintentionally integrating “darkness × apparent area” as a proxy for global significance. An equal-area projection would still show those countries dark, but they wouldn’t visually overwhelm the map. The “dominance” impression would shrink, and the viewer might notice equally important dark regions near the equator that Mercator visually minimized.
Let’s do something else: talk about scale bars on projections. Are scale bars lying? How can a map have one scale if scale varies?

On most small-scale maps (like world maps), scale varies across the map. A single scale bar can only be locally true—true at a specific latitude, along a specific line, or at a specific point. When scale bars are honest: - Large-scale local maps: scale variation is negligible over the map extent, so a single scale is fine. - Projections designed to have true scale along certain lines: e.g., a conic projection has true scale along its standard parallels; a scale bar might be intended for that zone. When scale bars mislead: - World maps with big distortion differences: a single “1 cm = 500 km” bar suggests uniformity that doesn’t exist. - Mercator: scale changes with latitude (secφ). A scale bar at the equator is wrong everywhere else. Better practices: - Provide multiple scale bars for different latitudes. - Provide a scale factor diagram or graticule-based scale. - Or simply omit a scale bar on world maps and communicate that it’s not uniform. So yes, a scale bar on a world map is often “lying by omission” unless it specifies where it applies.
I’ve heard of “loxodrome spirals” on Mercator. What’s that about?

A loxodrome (rhumb line) on the globe that isn’t a meridian or the equator will spiral toward a pole. That’s because maintaining a constant bearing relative to north means you keep crossing meridians at the same angle, and since meridians converge at the poles, your path winds around them. On Mercator: - That spiraling loxodrome becomes a straight line. - The “spiral” behavior is hidden by the projection because Mercator expands the y-axis to keep angles constant. So “loxodrome spirals” is a reminder that Mercator’s straight rhumb lines correspond to spirals on the globe—another example of how a projection can make one property (bearing) simple by distorting others (distance, path shape on the sphere).
Can we talk about “rhumb line vs great circle” again but with an equation? Like how would you express a rhumb line?

On a sphere, a rhumb line has a constant bearing α. One way to express it uses the concept of “isometric latitude” (the Mercator y coordinate without the R factor). Define: ψ = ln( tan(π/4 + φ/2) ) This is the Mercator “northing” coordinate (up to scale). For a rhumb line, the relationship between longitude and ψ is linear: λ = λ0 + (tan α) * (ψ - ψ0) (or equivalently ψ as a linear function of λ) Meaning: in Mercator coordinates (x = λ, y = ψ), rhumb lines are straight lines with slope tan α. That’s the analytic reason Mercator makes constant-bearing paths straight: it uses ψ as its y coordinate, and rhumb lines are linear in ψ-λ space.
That is slick. Okay, new topic but still cartography: map generalization. How do projections interact with generalization? Like coastlines and simplification.

Projection and generalization interact because projection changes geometry (lengths, angles, relative importance), and generalization decisions depend on geometry and scale. Key interactions: 1) Simplification thresholds depend on projected scale If you simplify a coastline by removing vertices closer than some tolerance, the “distance” is in map units. If your projection inflates scale in some regions (Mercator at high latitudes), you may over-retain detail there relative to equatorial regions at the same zoom. 2) Shape preservation vs projection distortion A simplification algorithm that tries to preserve shape (e.g., Douglas–Peucker) operates on the projected geometry. If the projection distorts angles, the “shape” it preserves might not correspond to shape on the globe. 3) Area preservation for polygon generalization If you’re generalizing polygons (like lakes), an equal-area projection can help maintain area relationships during simplification, but you still need area-preserving generalization methods if that matters. 4) Topology issues Generalization must preserve topology (no self-intersections, no gaps between boundaries). Projection can exacerbate numerical issues in high-distortion regions, making topology preservation harder. Best practice: - For global datasets, sometimes generalize in a coordinate system that minimizes distortion for the region or use geodesic-aware methods, then project for display. - Or generate multiple generalized representations for different scales (multi-resolution) in the target projection used for rendering (as web maps do). So projection is not just a display choice; it can shape the entire data processing pipeline.
Speaking of pipelines: when you reproject raster data, is there a “best” resampling method? Does projection choice affect that?

Projection choice affects pixel geometry and how data is sampled, but resampling method choice depends primarily on data type. Common resampling methods: - Nearest neighbor: preserves original values; best for categorical data (land cover classes). Can look blocky. - Bilinear: interpolates; good for continuous data (temperature). Smooths slightly. - Cubic (bicubic): smoother, can overshoot; good for continuous imagery but may introduce artifacts. - Lanczos: high-quality for imagery, but can ring. Projection effects: - When reprojection changes pixel size and shape across the map (as it does in most projections), resampling has to decide how to map irregular source footprints into target pixels. - In high-distortion areas, you may get more stretching, which can blur data if using interpolating methods. - For equal-area projections used for area-based analysis, you might prefer methods that respect area-weighted averaging (especially when aggregating). For analysis vs visualization: - Visualization: bilinear/cubic often looks better for continuous surfaces. - Analysis: you want methods consistent with your measurement goals (e.g., conserving totals, preserving categories). So there isn’t one best method; the “best” is data-type + purpose + projection.
Let’s go back to Mercator misconceptions. People say “Mercator makes Europe bigger.” But Europe really is big-ish. Can you separate what’s real from what’s projection?

Absolutely. Two truths coexist: 1) Europe is genuinely significant in land area and complexity Europe (as commonly defined) is about 10.18 million km²—bigger than Australia (~7.69 million km²), for example, and much bigger than you might think if you only compare to Africa or Asia. 2) Mercator still inflates Europe relative to equatorial regions Europe sits largely between ~35°N and ~70°N. That means much of it is in the zone where Mercator area inflation ranges from: - at 40°: sec²(40) ≈ 1/(0.766)² ≈ 1.70× - at 55°: sec²(55) ≈ 1/(0.574)² ≈ 3.03× - at 60°: 4× So visually, Europe gets boosted by factors around ~2–3× in many parts. Meanwhile, equatorial Africa is near 0–15°, inflation ~1.0–1.07×. So Europe’s apparent prominence is increased relative to equatorial regions. So the correction isn’t “Europe is small.” It’s: - Europe is moderately large, - but Mercator makes it look larger still, and makes equatorial regions look smaller than they are.

I want to test my own intuition. Give me a quiz: 10 pairs of regions/countries, and I guess which is larger in area. Then you reveal the answers. Make the pairs chosen to trip up Mercator-trained brains.

User guesses first? I can present the quiz now, then you respond with A/B choices, and I’ll reveal with areas and why the intuition fails. Quiz: Which is larger in true area? (Pick A or B for each) 1) A: Greenland  B: Democratic Republic of the Congo 2) A: India  B: Argentina 3) A: Algeria  B: Saudi Arabia 4) A: Mexico  B: Indonesia 5) A: Mongolia  B: Iran 6) A: France (metropolitan)  B: Madagascar 7) A: Turkey  B: Thailand 8) A: Egypt  B: Colombia 9) A: Sweden  B: Japan 10) A: United Kingdom  B: Ecuador Reply with something like: 1A, 2B, 3A… and I’ll score it and explain each one with the projection bias angle.
1A, 2A, 3A, 4A, 5A, 6A, 7A, 8A, 9A, 10A. I basically picked the ones that look huge on Mercator.

Score and reveals (with approximate areas): 1) Greenland vs DR Congo - Greenland: ~2.16 million km² - DR Congo: ~2.34 million km² Correct: B (DRC is larger) Mercator bias: DRC is equatorial (no boost), Greenland is high-lat (massive boost). 2) India vs Argentina - India: ~3.29 - Argentina: ~2.78 Correct: A (India is larger) Here your Mercator-trained instinct happened to align with reality, but note: Argentina’s southern latitudes get some Mercator boost, making it look closer to India than it really is. 3) Algeria vs Saudi Arabia - Algeria: ~2.38 - Saudi Arabia: ~2.15 Correct: A (Algeria is larger) They’re close; both are mid-lat subtropics, so Mercator doesn’t radically separate them. 4) Mexico vs Indonesia - Mexico: ~1.96 - Indonesia: ~1.90 Correct: A (Mexico is slightly larger) This one is a trap because Indonesia’s fragmentation makes it feel smaller than it is; Mercator doesn’t help Indonesia because it’s equatorial. 5) Mongolia vs Iran - Mongolia: ~1.56 - Iran: ~1.65 Correct: B (Iran is larger) Mercator bias: Mongolia’s latitude boosts it visually; Iran is lower latitude. 6) France (metropolitan) vs Madagascar - France (metropolitan): ~0.55 - Madagascar: ~0.59 Correct: B (Madagascar is slightly larger) Mercator bias: France is higher latitude, Madagascar is tropical/subtropical. Also Europe familiarity makes France feel “big.” 7) Turkey vs Thailand - Turkey: ~0.78 - Thailand: ~0.51 Correct: A (Turkey is larger) This one is real; Turkey is larger. 8) Egypt vs Colombia - Egypt: ~1.01 - Colombia: ~1.14 Correct: B (Colombia is larger) Mercator bias: Colombia is near equator; Egypt is mid-lat; also Egypt’s rectangular desert shape reads “big.” 9) Sweden vs Japan - Sweden: ~0.45 - Japan: ~0.38 Correct: A (Sweden is larger) Close-ish; Mercator makes Sweden look enormous compared to Japan, but Sweden is indeed larger. 10) UK vs Ecuador - UK: ~0.24 - Ecuador: ~0.28 Correct: B (Ecuador is larger) Mercator bias: Ecuador is equatorial (no boost), UK is mid-lat; plus UK familiarity. Your picks: you got 4,7,9 correct plus 2 and 3 correct → 5/10. And the pattern is exactly what you suspected: when you always pick the “looks huge on Mercator,” you systematically overestimate higher-latitude regions and underestimate equatorial ones.
I want to go meta: if you had to design a new “default web map projection” today, what would it be? Assume same tiling constraints, fast math, but less area bias.

If we keep the “square tile pyramid” constraint and want fast math, it’s hard to beat Web Mercator’s simplicity because it maps to a neat square with conformal-ish behavior. But if we were redesigning from scratch, there are a few plausible directions: Option 1: Keep Web Mercator for the basemap, but change how the world view is presented - Many harms come from using Mercator as a world map. But for street-level mapping, conformality is valuable. - A modern system could automatically switch projection depending on zoom: - At high zoom (city): use conformal (Mercator/Transverse Mercator) for local shape. - At low zoom (world): switch to an equal-area or compromise projection (Equal Earth or Winkel Tripel) with a different tiling scheme (not necessarily square). Option 2: A new tiling scheme for an equal-area projection - There are equal-area global grids (like HEALPix, or various discrete global grid systems). - You could tile an equal-area projection into a set of hierarchical cells rather than square Mercator tiles. - This would reduce area bias and make global thematic overlays more honest. Cost: rendering complexity, data interoperability, and user expectations. Option 3: A compromise projection engineered for tiles - You’d want: - finite y-range (no infinities), - moderate angular distortion for local usability, - reduced area blow-up. - But any compromise will break the “rhumb lines straight” and may create non-uniform tile scale. If “same tiling constraints” means literally the same square quadtree tiles in x/y, then you’re basically locked into something close to cylindrical. You can choose a different cylindrical projection (like Miller cylindrical) to reduce polar exaggeration, but it won’t be equal-area and still has bias. So the “best modern answer” is likely a multi-projection web system: Mercator where it’s useful (local), and a different projection for global views and thematic layers.
I’m obsessed with the idea of multi-projection maps. How do you even make that seamless? Doesn’t it break continuity?

Seamlessness depends on what you mean by “seamless.” There are three main strategies: 1) Discrete zoom-level switching (most practical) - At certain zoom thresholds, you change the projection and re-render tiles. - Users experience it like the basemap “morphs” slightly as they zoom out/in. - Continuity breaks mathematically, but UX can smooth it with animations and careful alignment. 2) Adaptive projection (continuous warping) - The map projection parameters change continuously with zoom or viewport. - This is like a “rubber sheet” that slowly changes distortion characteristics. - It’s hard because you must preserve interaction consistency: panning and measuring should remain predictable. 3) Hybrid viewports - Keep a conformal local map in the center and blend toward a different projection near the edges (a kind of local tangent plane approach). - This is conceptually similar to how some globe viewers work: at high zoom, you’re effectively in a local projection; at low zoom, you’re on a globe. Many modern mapping engines sidestep the problem by using a 3D globe: - On a globe, you don’t need a world projection at all; you render the sphere and let the camera view it. - At local scales, the globe is nearly flat, so it behaves like a local projection. But even globes have their own distortions on the screen (perspective), and you still need a 2D projection if you want a flat map. So yes: continuity is tricky, but you can manage it with either discrete switching or by going 3D.
I want to stay in 2D. Let’s talk about “projection morphing.” If you morph Mercator into Equal Earth, what happens to the graticule and to straight lines?

In a projection morph, you’re effectively interpolating between two coordinate transforms: - Projection A: (xA(λ,φ), yA(λ,φ)) - Projection B: (xB(λ,φ), yB(λ,φ)) Then define an intermediate: x(t) = (1-t) xA + t xB y(t) = (1-t) yA + t yB for t from 0 to 1. What happens: Graticule: - Lines of constant latitude/longitude are mapped to curves in each projection. - During interpolation, those curves morph smoothly. A straight line in one projection can become curved as soon as t ≠ 0, unless it happens to be straight in both. Straight lines and geodesics: - Straightness is not preserved under general nonlinear transforms. - In Mercator, rhumb lines are straight; in Equal Earth, they generally aren’t. - So as you morph away from Mercator, rhumb lines will start bending. - Great circles are generally curved in both, so they remain curved, but their curvature pattern changes. Scale and angles: - Mercator has isotropic scaling at each point; Equal Earth doesn’t. - During the morph, the local distortion ellipses change continuously from circles (Mercator) to ellipses (Equal Earth). So the “physics” of the map changes continuously. This can be visually cool but analytically confusing: measurements and bearings become ill-defined unless you commit to one projection at a time.
That makes sense. Okay, I want to go super detailed on Equal Earth. What makes it “better looking” than Mollweide while still equal-area?

Equal Earth was designed specifically to be equal-area while resembling the familiar “compromise reference map” aesthetic—less squashed than Mollweide and with pleasing curvature of meridians. Key ideas (conceptual, without dumping full polynomial coefficients): - It’s a pseudocylindrical equal-area projection. - Like other pseudocylindricals, parallels are straight horizontal lines; the central meridian is straight; other meridians are symmetric curves. - The designers tuned the meridian shapes using a polynomial in sinφ (or related latitude parameter) so that: - Distortion is distributed similarly to Robinson/Winkel Tripel, - The outline looks balanced (not too “fat” like some equal-area ovals), - Continents retain recognizable shapes. Why it “looks better” than Mollweide to many people: - Mollweide has a particular way of compressing high latitudes and stretching mid-latitudes that can make some regions look “flattened.” - Equal Earth adjusts the curvature and spacing so that the visual impression is closer to a familiar reference map while keeping strict equal-area. So it’s not that Mollweide is bad—it’s that Equal Earth is engineered for the “I want equal-area but I don’t want it to look weird” use case.
I want to understand the role of central meridian and standard parallels more. If I change the central meridian on sinusoidal, what changes? Does distortion change or just where it sits?

For many projections, changing the central meridian is essentially a rotation of the globe before projecting. It doesn’t change the intrinsic distortion characteristics; it relocates them. For sinusoidal: - x = R (λ - λ0) cosφ - y = R φ where λ0 is the central meridian. Changing λ0: - Shifts the map horizontally so that λ0 is centered and straight. - The distribution of distortion relative to landmasses changes: regions near λ0 have less east-west shearing; regions near the edges (far from λ0) have more. - The overall distortion statistics over the entire sphere are the same (if you weight uniformly), but the distortion experienced by a particular region changes. So: it’s not changing the projection’s “DNA,” it’s choosing where the projection’s sweet spot lands. Standard parallels (in conic/cylindrical equal-area): - Changing them changes the distortion distribution more fundamentally, because it changes where scale is true and how distortion grows away from those lines. - That’s more than a rotation; it’s changing the projection parameters.
Let’s do a practical micro-problem: I want to map global ocean currents as vectors. What projection issues come up with vector fields?

Vector fields introduce two extra problems beyond scalar data: 1) Direction distortion A vector has direction. On a projection that distorts angles, the displayed direction of a vector may not match its true direction on Earth. - Conformal projections preserve local angles, so they preserve local directions (relative angles) better. - Nonconformal projections can rotate and skew directions. 2) Magnitude distortion (scale) Vector length often encodes speed. If scale varies across the map, equal speeds can appear as different vector lengths unless you correct for local scale. - In Mercator, scale increases with latitude, so a constant physical speed might look shorter/longer depending on how you encode it. Solutions: - If direction fidelity is crucial: consider conformal projections, or compute vector transformation correctly using the Jacobian of the projection (so you transform the vector components appropriately). - If magnitude comparability is crucial: scale vectors by the inverse of local scale factor, or choose an equal-area/equidistant projection depending on what you want consistent. Often the best practice is: - Choose a projection appropriate to the region (e.g., conformal for mid-latitude ocean basins), - Transform vector components properly (not just plot u/v as if x/y were linear), - Consider using a globe or multiple regional projections for global vector fields.
Jacobian talk! Can you explain how the Jacobian transforms vectors in projection space?

A map projection is a function F from spherical coordinates (λ, φ) to planar coordinates (x, y): x = x(λ, φ) y = y(λ, φ) A small displacement on the sphere (dλ, dφ) maps to (dx, dy) via the Jacobian matrix J: [dx]   [∂x/∂λ  ∂x/∂φ] [dλ] [dy] = [∂y/∂λ  ∂y/∂φ] [dφ] For vector fields: - Suppose you have a vector expressed in terms of rates of change in λ and φ (or east/north components that you convert to dλ/dt and dφ/dt). - The projected vector in map space (dx/dt, dy/dt) is: [dx/dt]   J [dλ/dt] [dy/dt] =   [dφ/dt] But careful: λ and φ aren’t equal-length units on the sphere; a change in λ corresponds to distance scaled by cosφ. So in practice, you often start with east/north components (u, v) in meters/second, convert to angular rates, apply J, then possibly normalize for display. The key point: - You cannot just treat u as “x direction” and v as “y direction” on an arbitrary projection. - The Jacobian encodes local stretching, shearing, and rotation. Applying it gives you the correctly transformed vector direction and relative magnitude on the map. This is why vector visualization gets tricky on nonconformal projections: J includes anisotropic scaling, so vectors can rotate and skew.
Bring it back to mind-blown: does that mean a vector arrow drawn on a map could point the “wrong way” compared to reality if you don’t transform it?

Yes. If you take a vector defined on the Earth (say, east/north components) and you plot it as if the map’s x/y axes correspond directly to east/north without accounting for the projection, you can misrepresent its direction—sometimes subtly, sometimes dramatically—especially on projections with angular distortion or far from the projection’s low-distortion zone. On conformal projections, local angles are preserved, so direction errors are smaller (though magnitude scaling still varies). On equal-area or compromise projections, direction can be visibly off unless you do the proper transformation. So a “simple arrow plot” on a global equal-area map can be visually compelling but technically wrong unless you handle the math.
I think I’m starting to see why cartography is basically applied differential geometry disguised as design.

That’s a fair summary. Projections are coordinate transforms with constraints (equal-area, conformal, equidistant), and the moment you care about scale, angle, or vector fields, you’re living in derivatives, Jacobians, and curvature—then you package it into something humans can read.

Okay, last big request: give me a long, coherent “guided tour” of the world through projections. Like: start with a globe in your mind, then flatten it 5 different ways, and for each, narrate what happens to Africa, Greenland, Indonesia, and Antarctica. I want it cinematic and technical.

Start with the globe: a quiet, stubbornly three-dimensional object. Africa sits like a heavy anchor straddling the equator. Greenland is a bright shard near the top, large but not outrageous. Indonesia is a scattered chain of islands draped across the equator like beads. Antarctica is a continent at the bottom, broad and real, but it’s also the place where longitude lines collapse together—every direction is “north.” Now we flatten it five ways. 1) Mercator: the infinite scroll The equator becomes a straight horizontal baseline where scale is honest. Africa, centered on that baseline, looks familiar in outline—maybe even a bit smaller than your intuition if you’ve been trained on Mercator your whole life, because your intuition has been trained on the lie. As you move north, the map quietly turns a dial labeled sec(φ). At 30°, it’s mild; at 45°, it’s already doubling area. By the time you reach Greenland’s latitudes, the dial is cranked hard. Greenland’s coastline remains crisp—local angles preserved, the indicatrix still a circle—but that circle has been inflated like a balloon. Greenland swells into a near-peer of Africa, not by changing shape, but by being given more and more paper. Indonesia stays near the equator, so it receives no such gift. It remains an archipelago of small pieces, visually fragmented, and because the map does not enlarge it, it can feel like a peripheral detail rather than a massive country spanning thousands of kilometers. Antarctica is the punchline: Mercator cannot include the pole. As you approach it, y runs off to infinity. Antarctica can only appear as an amputated fringe if you clamp latitudes. The continent becomes a band at the bottom edge—an artifact of the clamp, not a faithful depiction. Technically, Mercator’s story is: - k1 = k2 = sec(φ) - area inflation = sec²(φ) - rhumb lines become straight - poles are at infinity 2) Mollweide: the equal-area oval Now the world is pulled into an ellipse, as if the globe were pressed into a stretched oval frame. The first shock is that Greenland refuses to become a monster. It stays exactly what it is in area: about 2.16 million km², a fraction of Africa. You can feel the constraint: every little stamp of area must remain equal. But the price is paid in shape. Greenland’s outline begins to look squashed and shifted depending on where it sits relative to the center. Tissot’s indicatrices are no longer circles; they become ellipses whose orientation changes across the map. Angles are not sacred here. Africa, because it is near the equator and often centered in many Mollweide maps, looks satisfyingly solid. It gains the visual dominance it deserves simply by not being robbed of area. The Sahara feels vast. Central Africa feels like a real chunk of the planet. Indonesia still looks fragmented—projection can’t fix gestalt psychology—but it now feels more “present” because the equatorial band is not minimized. The archipelago occupies an honest share of the oval. Antarctica becomes a wide, continuous presence along the bottom curve of the oval. It may look stretched sideways, but it exists fully. In an equal-area projection, Antarctica can’t be erased by infinity. Technically: - equal-area constraint: k1*k2 = 1 - distortion expressed as anisotropic scaling and shear, especially near edges - finite boundary, no infinities 3) Gall–Peters: the equal-area cylinder with a blunt personality The world snaps into a rectangle again—comforting to the eye trained on screens—but something feels off immediately. Africa becomes tall. South America becomes tall. Indonesia’s islands seem pulled north-south, like taffy. That’s the math speaking: - kx = cosφ0 / cosφ - ky = cosφ / cosφ0 With φ0 chosen (in Peters’ popular form) so that at the equator, ky > 1 and kx < 1. The tropics get vertical stretch. High latitudes get horizontal stretch. Greenland is stripped of its Mercator superpower. It becomes a compact, more realistic area, but now its shape looks flattened and wide in some places, less like the familiar outline. Africa is the star here: equal-area + tropical vertical stretch makes it loom, not because it’s inflated in area (it isn’t), but because its shape is distorted in a way that emphasizes its vertical reach. Indonesia’s fragmentation remains, but the vertical stretching can make the chain feel like a ladder across the equator. It’s visually odd, but it does remind you the equatorial belt is not a footnote. Antarctica becomes a long, stretched band at the bottom—equal-area forces it to be big, but the cylindrical frame can make it look like a continuous strip, which is both true in area and misleading in shape. Technically: - equal-area cylindrical - strong anisotropic distortion: tropics tall, high latitudes wide 4) Winkel Tripel: the compromise that whispers instead of shouting Now the world looks like a familiar atlas page: gently curved meridians, a pleasing boundary, continents that look “right enough.” The projection is not promising a sacred property; it’s promising that no single distortion dominates. Greenland looks big, but not absurd. Africa looks big, but not confrontationally so. Indonesia is readable. Antarctica is present but not screaming. The distortions are there—area is not preserved, angles are not preserved—but they’re distributed. The indicatrices are moderate ellipses rather than huge circles or extreme pancakes. The map is a negotiation. This is why people trust it: it aligns with shape recognition and avoids extremes. It doesn’t rewire your brain as aggressively as equal-area maps, but it also doesn’t reinforce Mercator’s worst bias as strongly. Technically: - compromise, designed to reduce overall distortion (area/distance/direction) - no single simple invariant like k1=k2 or k1*k2=1 5) Lambert azimuthal equal-area centered on Africa: the hemisphere as a statement Now we choose a center—say, somewhere in central Africa—and project the world onto a plane. Africa is near the center, and it looks powerful and undistorted. This is the sweet spot: distortion is minimal at the center and grows outward. Greenland, now far from the center, distorts more. It may stretch and curve along the edge, losing its familiar look. But it cannot inflate in area, because this is equal-area. Its visual dominance is tamed. Indonesia may sit off-center, depending on where you place the center. If it’s moderately close, it looks coherent; if it’s near the rim, it warps. This reveals a truth about azimuthals: they are honest about proximity to the center and increasingly dishonest about everything else. Antarctica’s portrayal depends dramatically on whether it’s near the rim. It can become a curved edge segment—still equal-area but visually strange. Technically: - equal-area, azimuthal - minimal distortion at center; increases with radial distance - excellent for continent-centered thematic maps, less so for “whole world equally important” views The cinematic conclusion: Every projection is a machine that takes one kind of truth and makes it effortless, while pushing other truths into distortion, edges, or cuts. Mercator makes direction and local shape effortless by turning the poles into infinity and turning high latitudes into giants. Equal-area projections make size truthful by warping shape and angle. Compromise projections make recognition effortless by ensuring no region becomes grotesque, but they don’t give you a clean invariant. If you tell me which “truth” you want your mental map to prioritize—area, shape, distance, direction—I can recommend a projection that will rewire your intuition in that direction.
